segments.s163-g65: This clause is a reporting clause: چِنین گفت رستم (literally “thus said Rostam”). که introduces the content clause that follows: 'that this is the counsel.' اینست is the present/nominal copula 'this is' used in fixed phrase form; رای is the predicative noun 'opinion/counsel.' Word order is Subject (رستم) after the verb گفت (common in Persian poetry) and then the complement introduced by که.
segments.s163-g66: جزین (جز + این) = 'except by this / except in this way' begins a limiting phrase. روی here functions idiomatically as 'manner/way' (poetic use) rather than literally 'face,' and پیمان نیاید بجای is a negative verbal phrase: نیاید (present/subjunctive-like negative of آمدن) + بجای 'into its place' meaning 'is not brought/does not come to fulfilment.' Together: 'no covenant will come into place except by this manner.'
segments.s163-g67: به شبگیر is a temporal/characterizing phrase 'as a night-visiting/at night' (شبگیر = night-visitor/nocturnal). گرسیوز is the subject and آمد is the past tense verb 'came.' بدر is a simile adverbial 'like the full moon' placed after the verb for poetic effect. The clause gives the arrival and its luminous simile.
segments.s163-g68: چنان چون بود is a redundant poetic pair meaning 'just as/such as he was' (چنان ... چون = 'in that manner'), with بود as the past copula 'was.' با + nouns (کلاه و کمر) marks accompaniment/attire: 'with hat and girdle' (کمر = belt/girdle). The whole segment describes his appearance as he came.
segments.s163-g69: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came' (poetic preposed form). به پیش سیاوش = 'into the presence of Siavash' (به + پیش = 'to/in front of'). زمین is the direct object of حسنِ ادب (he kissed the ground) but here it sits at the end of the clause to mark the act of obeisance: 'came before Siavash (and) the ground...' — the next verb completes the action.
segments.s163-g70: ببوسید is the simple past 'he kissed' (here understood as kissing the ground in obeisance). و links the two actions. بر شاه کرد آفرین is an idiomatic object-verb order: بر شاه 'toward/on the king' (meaning 'to the king') + کرد (did/made) + آفرین (an acclamation/praise). Literally 'he made acclamation upon the king' = 'he praised/applauded the king.'
segments.s163-g71: سیاوش بدو گفت = 'Siavash said to him' (سیاوش subject, بدو = 'to him'). کز is the archaic/poetic compound of که + از meaning 'that/from which' and here introduces the reason clause: کز کار تو = 'because of / concerning your deed/action.' Word order places the reason immediately after the reporting phrase.
segments.s163-g72: پراندیشه بودم is an adjectival-predicate phrase: پراندیشه 'full of thought' in older usage often means 'anxious/worried' and بودم is the first-person past 'I was.' ز گفتار تو = 'because of / from your speech' (ز = poetic 'from/because of', گفتار تو = 'your words'). Together: 'I have been worried because of what you said.'
segments.s163-g73: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. رای یکسر = 'a wholly/entire decision' (رای = plan/decision, یکسر = entirely). بران is an archaic locutive/particle 'on/thereon' linking to the following clause. شد is the past/become verb 'was made', and درست is a predicative adjective 'right/proper' describing the decision.
segments.s163-g74: که introduces the content/purpose clause 'that...'. از کینه = 'of/from hatred' (کینه = hatred). دل را is the definite object marker construction ('the heart' as direct object). بخواهیم is present-subjunctive/optative 'we should/would wish' (classical usage), and شست is an archaic verbal form of 'to wash' used here as the action to be carried out.
segments.s163-g75: تو is the emphatic subject pronoun 'you'. پاسخ is the object 'a reply/answer'. فرستی is the 2nd-person past (classical) of 'to send' — 'you sent'. به افراسیاب is the prepositional object 'to Afrasiab'. The order (subject–object–verb) is normal in older Persian.
segments.s163-g76: که introduces the reported content: 'that...'. از کین = 'from/of vengeance' (کین = vengeance/hatred). اگر شد is the conditional 'if it comes to pass / if it should happen' (شد used for 'happen/take effect'). سرت literally 'your head' (here 'upon you'), and پر شتاب is a predicate 'full of haste' (پر = full (of), شتاب = haste). Note the classical placement of اگر inside the clause.
segments.s163-g77: کسی = 'someone/anyone' (indefinite subject). کاو is an archaic relative-form meaning 'who/that' (contracted from 'که او'); it introduces the relative clause. ببیند is present/subjunctive 'sees/foresees'. سرانجام بد = 'a bad outcome/end' (سرانجام = outcome, بد = bad).
segments.s163-g78: ز کردار بد = 'from/because of bad conduct' (ز = from/poetic). بازگشتش is a nominalized verbal phrase 'his returning/turning back' (بازگشت + -ش 'his'), i.e. 'that he turn back'. سزد is the impersonal/predicate 'it befits / it is fitting', so together the clause means 'it is fitting that he turn away from bad deeds.'
segments.s163-g79: دلی here denotes 'a heart' (classical/dialectal form of دل). کز is the relative/prepositional 'that/from which', introducing the qualifier. خرد = 'wisdom'. گردد is the present/subjunctive/passive-like 'becomes/is made'. آراسته is the participial/adjectival 'adorned/refined' describing the heart.
segments.s163-g80: یکی = 'one/a single' (here emphatic 'a single/true'). گنج = 'treasure'. گردد = 'becomes'. پر از خواسته = 'full of desire(s)' (پر = full, از links the filler, خواسته = wish/desire). The phrase reads 'becomes a single treasure, full of desires.'
segments.s163-g201: This is a simple narrative clause in V + (prepositional) complement + comment order: بخندید (past, 3rd‑sg) 'he laughed' followed by و 'and' and با او 'to/with him' (here 'to him') and سپس چنین گفت شاه where گفت (here rendered as 'said') takes the subject شاه placed after the verb (common in older Persian). So: 'He laughed and to him thus said the king.'
segments.s163-g202: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that is'), and چاره 'remedy/solution' is the noun being asked about. به از is a comparative construction ('more/better than'), formed by به + از. جنگ‌ای is the poetic sequence where ای is the vocative particle attached (addressing someone as 'O ...') and نیک‌خواه is the vocative addressee 'well‑wisher' or 'kind friend'. Overall: 'That [is], what remedy is better than war, O well‑wisher.'
segments.s163-g203: و دیگر 'and again/besides' links to what follows. کزان = از + آن 'from which/therefrom' (relative/locative reference to the prior matter), خوابم here means 'my dream' (خواب = dream) and آمد is past 'came' (an event happened to me). نهیب is a sudden loud cry/clamor — so the whole means 'and again from that a cry came to my dream' (i.e. I had a startling cry in a dream).
segments.s163-g204: ز بالا 'from on high' (ز as poetic 'from'), بدیدم is the past perfective 'I saw' (1st‑sg), نشان 'a sign/token' and نشیب 'descent' or falling — so: 'From above I saw a sign of descent.' Word order is prepositional phrase + verb + object, typical in narrative past.
segments.s163-g205: پر از درد گشتم is 'I became full of pain' (پر = full + از = of; گشتم = گشتن in past, 'I became/was turned'), سوی چاره باز places the motion/attention 'toward the remedy again' (سوی = toward, چاره = remedy, باز = again). The clause describes a state/result followed by a movement of attention/action.
segments.s163-g206: بدان here functions as 'to that / toward that' (ب = to/into prefix; دان in older usage relates to 'that'/'regard'), and تا introduces purpose ('so that' / in order that). نبینم is the negative subjunctive/present (ن‑ prefix + بینم) expressing purpose/result 'that I may not see'. نشیب و فراز 'downs and ups' are the objects of نبینم. So: 'to that (i.e. I turned) so that I would not behold descent and ascent.'
segments.s163-g207: به گنج و درم چاره آراستم is a past active clause: به گنج و درم 'with treasure and dirhams/money' (به as 'with/using'), چاره 'a remedy/solution' is the object, and آراستم (آراستن, past 1st‑sg) 'I arranged/prepared' — literal: 'I arranged a remedy with treasure and money.'
segments.s163-g208: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame; شد is past 'became/turned out'; بران سان = 'in that manner/thereupon' (بران = 'on/thereupon', سان = 'such/so'), که introduces the relative clause 'that I wished' (من خواستم). So: 'Now it became/turned out in that way which I wanted.'
segments.s163-g25: یکی is an indefinite determiner here ('a certain' / 'one'), placed before the noun یادگاری (the direct object). یادگاری is the thing sent ('memento'). به + نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/near the king' (نزدیک used adjectivally/adverbially); in older Persian constructions like this, به + نزدیک means 'to the presence of / toward the king.' Word order is object phrase before the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s163-g26: فرستاد is a past-tense transitive verb 'he dispatched/sent' (honorific or narrative past). با من functions as an instrumental/accompaniment phrase here ('with me' / 'entrusted to me'), indicating the speaker was the carrier; کنون is an adverb 'now' modifying the following locative phrase. در به راه is an older locative phrase equivalent to modern در راه / به راه meaning 'on the road' or 'on the way.' The verb governs the earlier object (یادگاری) which is understood to have been sent with the speaker.
segments.s163-g27: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb used for a ruler or important person — here 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces the result/subordinate clause ('so that' / 'and then'), followed by پرده (the object, 'curtain') and برداشتند (past plural 'they lifted/removed'). The sequence is main verb (he commanded) + تا + subordinate clause showing the action that followed.
segments.s163-g28: به چشم سیاووش is a prepositional phrase 'to/before Siavosh's eye(s)' with به marking the target; چشم here means 'eye' or 'sight' and stands for the immediate perceptual focus. بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/put' (ب‑ prefix is an archaic/classical variant alongside گذاشتند). The agent 'they' (attendants) is expressed by the verb ending, the object (the curtain or item) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s163-g29: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'; it begins a range that is completed by تا ('to/as far as'). دروازه شهر is 'the city gate' and تا بارگاه means 'to the palace/royal court.' The construction ز ... تا ... marks spatial extent ('from X to Y').
segments.s163-g30: درم بود is an existential clause: درم ('dirham', i.e. money/coins) followed by بود (past of 'to be'), 'there was money.' The following nouns (اسپ, غلام, کلاه) are coordinated with و and are to be taken as additional items in the same existential list; the verb بود applies to the whole series even though it only appears once after the first item, a common elliptical pattern in Persian listing.
segments.s163-g31: کس here functions negatively ('no one' in older Persian contexts) combined with نشناخت (past negative sense 'did not know / could not recognize/measure'), so the clause says no one knew/estimated اندازه ('measure/extent') of آنرا ('it'). که چند introduces the content of the unknown measure ('how many / how much'). Word order places the negated verb before the clause specifying what was unknown.
segments.s163-g32: ز before دینار is the poetic preposition 'of/from' or part of the expression indicating measure in dinars ('in dinars'). وز here is best read as an archaic/compounded form meaning 'and (in) weight/amount' (sense 'weight / burden'), joining the following items تاج و تخت بلند ('crown and lofty throne') as additional measures of value. بلند is a postpositive adjective 'lofty/high' modifying تخت (and implicitly تاج). The whole phrase completes the question introduced by که چند: how many dinars, and how much in crown and high throne.
segments.s163-g89: و is the coordinating 'and'. دیگر here means 'other/else' (i.e., 'whatever else'). ز is the poetic form of از 'of/from' and combines with ایران‌زمین 'the land of Iran'. هرچ هست literally 'whatever exists' (هرچ = 'whatever'; هست = present 'exists'). The whole segment states 'and whatever else exists in the land of Iran.'
segments.s163-g90: که is the relative 'that/which' linking to the previous clause. آن شهرها را is a topical/definite noun phrase ('those cities') marked with را. تو داری is second-person present/future 'you have/hold/possess'. به دست is a prepositional phrase literally 'in the hand' — an idiom meaning 'under your control.' Together: 'that those cities you hold in your hand.'
segments.s163-g91: بپردازی is a classical/poetic present/future form with the b‑prefix indicating an action to take place ('you will depart/turn away'); the exact lexical shade is archaic, so sense mapping is uncertain. و خود 'and you yourself' (خود is emphatic). به توران شوی uses شوی (from شدن) 'you become/go' with به marking direction/place — 'you will go/become in Turan.' The clause presents two coordinated actions: depart and go to Turan.
segments.s163-g92: زمانی is a temporal adverb 'for a while' or 'for a time'. ز جنگ و ز کین is a pair of complements introduced by poetic ز = 'from/because of' meaning 'from war and from hatred'. بغنوی (from غنود) here means 'you will fall into slumber/abate' or more figuratively 'you will cease/let up' — the verb agrees with the same subject as the previous clause (implicit 'you').
segments.s163-g93: نباشد is a negative existential/coprial form 'there is not / let there not be' (classical usage). جز 'except', از راستی 'of/from truth' functioning as the limiting phrase, در میان 'in/among/between' — together: 'There shall be nothing in between except truth.'
segments.s163-g94: به کینه 'with/for hatred' (به marking the relation), نبندم is the 1st person present/future negative of بستن: 'I will not bind/fasten/put on'. کمر here means 'girdle/belt' (literally the waist-girdle) and is used idiomatically ('gird one's loins' = prepare oneself). بر میان 'on/against the middle/between (us)'. The clause says 'I will not gird my loins with hatred between (us),' i.e. I will not take up hatred between us.
segments.s163-g95: فرستم is 1st person singular 'I send' / 'I will send' from فرستادن. یکی is 'a certain' (indefinite quantifier) modifying نامه 'letter'. نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king' = 'to the king's presence'. This is a simple transitive clause: 'I will send a certain letter to the king.'
segments.s163-g96: مگر here functions as 'unless' / 'except' (classical usage). بشتی literally 'rear' (the rear part or rear-guard) is the likely subject or topical noun. باز can mean 'again' or 'open' and here qualifies the action that follows. خواند is 3rd person past/simple 'he/she/it read/recited' (classical Persian often uses past forms where modern would use present perfect); سپاه 'the army' is the object or appositive. A literal gloss is 'unless a rear (basti) of the army read aloud' — the precise pragmatic nuance is idiomatic/elliptical in the verse.
segments.s163-g185: از ایران‌زمین (prepositional phrase) = 'from/out of the land of Iran.' بسپرد is a third-person optative/jussive form (classical/poetic) of سپردن used here as an exhortation/optative meaning 'let it be sent away/consigned' or 'may it be driven off.' تیره خاک is the patient/object ('dark soil' or 'black/dank earth') placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s163-g186: بشوید is a present/subjunctive passive-like form of شدن meaning 'may it become / may it be washed' (optative: 'let it be cleansed'). دل is the grammatical subject ('the heart'). از + (کینه و جنگ) indicates separation/removal ('from hatred and war'), and پاک is the predicate adjective 'clean/pure.' Word order follows classical Persian preference for predicate after subject for metrical reasons.
segments.s163-g187: ز خویشان = 'from/among his relatives' (ز used poetically as 'from/among'). فرستاد is simple past 'he sent.' صد is the numeral 'a hundred' (direct object of فرستاد). نزد من = 'to me / in my presence' (نزد + pronoun indicates destination or being brought to the speaker). The sequence reflects a compact classical order: source, verb, quantity, destination.
segments.s163-g188: بدین خواهش is the fixed classical phrase 'with this request' (بدین = 'with/by this' — here written as ب + دین in the segment list, functioning together). آمد is past 'came' (here 'came/appeared' with that request). گو is a quotative/participial device meaning 'saying' or 'thus speaking,' and پیلتن is the name of the speaker/agent. Together: 'Piltan came with this request, saying...'.
segments.s163-g189: گر introduces a conditional 'if.' او را is the object with the definite/accusative marker را ('him'). ببخشد is present/subjunctive (classical) of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'forgive' (optative/conditional: 'if he forgives him'). ز مهرش 'from/out of his affection' gives the reason or motive. سزاست is a fixed predicate 'it is fitting/just/deserved.'
segments.s163-g190: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/because.' The line uses inverted, emphatic word order for poetic effect: literally 'on/for his love his face on is witness' — i.e. 'his face bears witness to his love.' بر here means 'upon/regarding,' مهر او = 'his love/affection,' چهر او = 'his face,' and گواست is a contracted/classical form meaning 'is witness' (equivalent to modern گواه است). The repetition of بر is a rhetorical inversion to fit meter.
segments.s163-g191: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بنوشت here functions as the past tense 'wrote' (classical poetic form of نوشتن). نامه is the direct object 'letter.' یل جنگجوی is an appositive/epithet 'the valiant / warlike hero' qualifying the writer; classical Persian often places the object and its modifier in this compact order for metrical reasons, so this chunk reads 'when the warrior-hero wrote the letter.'
segments.s163-g192: سوی شاه کاووس = 'toward King Kavus' (سوی = 'towards/to the presence of'). بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put/turned' and here collocates with روی to mean 'he turned/placed his face (toward),' i.e., 'he presented/approached.' روی is 'face' (here 'countenance/direction' = 'he faced the king'). The verb thus indicates approaching the king and presenting the letter.
segments.s163-g161: نخست is an adverb ('first, at first') that sets the sequence. آفرین کرد is a two-word verbal phrase: آفرین (here a noun/nominal interjection meaning 'praise' or 'acclamation') plus کرد (the past tense verb 'did/made'), together 'praised'. بر marks the target of the praise ('toward/to'), and دادگر is the definite target 'the just one' (here a just ruler). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal adverb → verb phrase → preposition + object.
segments.s163-g162: کزو is a fused relative/pronominal adverb ('from whom / from which') that links back to 'دادگر'. دید is the past tense 'he saw'. نیروی و فر و هنر are a coordinated list of direct objects ('strength, farr (royal glory), and skill/arts') that the speaker says he saw in that just one; the conjunction و simply links the nouns.
segments.s163-g163: This noun phrase lists the agent and the things given: خداوند ('the Lord' or 'God') is the subject, followed by a series of objects joined by و: هوش (intellect), زمان (time), و مکان (and place). Classical Persian often places the subject before a coordinated object list like this.
segments.s163-g164: خرد پروراند همی is a verb phrase: خرد ('wisdom') is the object, پروراند is the past tense verb 'nurtured/caused to grow', and همی is the classical continuous/affirmative particle (here 'indeed/always' or a poetical marker indicating ongoing action). با روان ('with the soul') is an adjunct showing accompaniment — i.e. wisdom was nurtured together with the soul.
segments.s163-g165: گذر نیست کس را is a negative existential construction: گذر ('passage, passage/escape') plus نیست ('is not') gives 'there is no passing/escape'. کس را uses کس ('anyone') with the object/topic marker را (here marking the affected person). ز فرمان او ('from his command') specifies what cannot be passed — 'from his command/decree'.
segments.s163-g166: This clause repeats the idea with a relative structure: کسی ('anyone') introduces the indefinite, but کاو here functions as a classical fused relative/particle ('who/that from him' / 'whoever'—an archaic usage not captured by the given lexical senses), so its sense is irregular. بگردد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن ('to turn/turn away' or 'to happen'), giving 'would turn' or 'turn away'. ز پیمان او ('from his covenant') completes the clause. Overall: 'there is no one who would turn away from his covenant.'
segments.s163-g167: ز گیتی نبیند مگر کاستی is a clause with an experiential verb: ز گیتی ('from the world') sets the domain, نبیند is a present/subjunctive/poetic form of 'see' meaning 'he sees' or 'perceives not', مگر ('except') introduces the exception, and کاستی ('lack, deficiency') is the only thing seen. The word order places the domain first and the verb before its exception, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s163-g168: بدو باشد افزونی و راستی: بدو ('to him' or 'for him') is a dative pronoun clitic phrase, باشد is the classical present/subjunctive copula meaning 'there is / may there be' (here used as a predictive or assertive 'there will be' / 'it is for him'), and افزونی و راستی ('increase and truth/uprightness') are the two things ascribed to him. The particle-based verb tense and the dative fronting are typical in Ferdowsi's poetic register.
segments.s163-g49: A short clause with the noun subject طلایه (vanguard) fronted; ز هر سو is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' modifying the verb. برون is an adverb 'out/forth' placed before the past-tense verb تاختند (3rd person plural past of تاختن), which here means 'rushed/charged.'
segments.s163-g50: چنان چون is an emphatic correlative meaning 'in such a way / as was fitting.' ببایست is an archaic modal form (present/past sense: 'it was necessary / ought') and functions parenthetically; برساختند is the past 3rd-plural 'they built/formed/raised' (object omitted, understood from context). The phrase thus means 'they arranged/formed themselves as was necessary.'
segments.s163-g51: Word order: proper name سیاوش (subject) + the poetic preposition ز with رستم to mark the addressee/object of asking ('asked of Rostam'). بپرسید is the past 3rd-singular of پرسیدن 'asked'. و connects to گفت, which introduces the reported command/speech.
segments.s163-g52: که introduces the content clause (that / the following command). این راز is the direct object 'this secret'; بیرون + کنید is a causative/periphrastic construction where کنید (2nd person plural/polite subjunctive) with بیرون means 'bring out / reveal.' از نهفت literally 'from hiding' (نهفت 'hidden'). The whole clause is a reported imperative: 'bring this secret out of concealment.'
segments.s163-g53: The clause is an interrogative nominal sentence: این آشتی جستن ('this seeking of reconciliation') is a noun phrase, followed by the locution از بهر 'for the sake/purpose of' and the copula-question چی ست 'what is (it)?' The initial که here functions as a rhetorical opener ('for what / why').
segments.s163-g54: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/attend/consider' (نگه = gaze/watch, کن = do!, imperative). که introduces the clause to be considered: تریاک این زهر چی ست 'what is the antidote/remedy to this poison' — note classical Persian often places the remedy word before the possessed phrase (تریاک [for] این زهر). Here تریاک is best understood as 'antidote/remedy' in context; چی ست at the end is the interrogative 'what is it?'.
segments.s163-g55: A prepositional/ablative phrase introduced by poetic ز ('from/on account of' or 'with regard to/seeing'), modifying the following imperative: پیوسته modifies خون 'continuous/ongoing blood' and به نزدیک اوی places it 'near him' (اوی = archaic 3rd-person pronoun). Together this gives the scene-setting adverbial 'from the continuous blood near him / seeing the continuous blood at his side.'
segments.s163-g56: ببین is an imperative 'see/look/observe.' تا here introduces the content/result clause ('so that' / 'to the effect that') and is often used with verbs of perception to mean 'see which...' کدامند is the interrogative-plural 'which/which ones are (they)?' and صد نامجوی is the quantified noun phrase 'the hundred name-seekers' (نامجوی = those who seek a name/fame).
segments.s163-g169: ازو باد بر شهریار آفرین is an optative/exclamatory phrase. ازو (a poetic zabt of از + او) marks the beneficiary/target ('for/to him'), باد is the optative particle 'may/let there be', بر + شهریار means 'upon the king', and آفرین is a noun/nominal exclamation 'praise/acclamation'. Word order places the beneficiary and optative particle before the phrase receiving the blessing.
segments.s163-g170: This is a compact nominal phrase describing the king: جهاندار 'world‑holder, sovereign' followed by وز نامداران گزین — here وز functions as a poetic connective meaning roughly 'and/from' (classical texts sometimes compress و + از), نامداران 'the famed/renowned ones', and گزین is adjectival/participial 'chosen' (i.e. 'chosen from the illustrious'). The phrase remains nominal and appositive to شهریار.
segments.s163-g171: رسیده is a perfect/passive participial form 'has arrived/reached'; به marks direction/target 'to', هر نیک و بد qualifies رای — 'every good and bad counsel/opinion', and او is the possessive/recipient 'him'. The whole clause means 'every good and bad opinion/counsel has reached him.'
segments.s163-g172: ستودن here functions as a noun 'praise' (verbal noun), خرد 'wisdom' is its genitive modifier, گشته is perfect/passive 'has become' or 'has been made', بالای او 'over him/above him' shows the locus of that status. Together: 'the praise of wisdom had become above him' (i.e. wisdom's praise was exalted upon him).
segments.s163-g173: رسیدم is 1st person past 'I arrived/reached'; به introduces the place 'to/at Balkh', و links the coordinate phrase, and به خرم بهار is a temporal/qualifying phrase 'in the joyful spring' (خرم qualifying بهار). The clause is straightforward S‑V‑O/adjunct order with the temporal/location phrases after the verb.
segments.s163-g174: همه is an adverb/quantifier 'entirely/altogether', شادمان 'happy' is predicative, بودم is past copula 'I was', از introduces cause/reason 'because of', and روزگار is the cause/subject 'the times/fate'. The clause reads 'I was wholly joyful because of the times.'
segments.s163-g175: ز (poetic variant of از) + من 'from/about me' with چون used as a conjunction 'when/as' and خبر یافت 'he received/heard the news' (یافت past 'found/obtained'). The whole clause means 'when Afrasiab got news about me.'
segments.s163-g176: سیه شد is a two‑word predicate 'became dark/blackened' (سیه = dark, شد = became). به چشم اندرش is a possessive locative: به چشم 'to/for the eye', اندر + ش (enclitic) 'in his' — together 'in his eyes' — and آفتاب is the subject 'the sun'. Literal order is 'dark became the sun in his eyes' = 'the sun grew dark in his eyes.'
segments.s163-g1: بیاورد is a third-person past form meaning “he brought” (classical Persian past tense). The order is verb–subject–object (بیاورد گرسیوز آن خواسته) where the verb comes first for emphasis; آن خواسته is the direct object “that request/wish.”
segments.s163-g2: This is a relative clause introduced by که “that/which.” روی زمین = “the face/surface of the earth.” ز + و here represent the older fused expression زου (از او) meaning “by/from it/him”; the text was tokenized into ز and و but together act as a preposition + clitic pronoun ('by it'). شد آراسته is a passive/casual verbal idea “became/adorned.”
segments.s163-g3: دامان (literally “skirt/hem” here used topographically) + تا + لب رود جیحون = “as far as the bank/edge of the Jeyhun river.” تا is used in the sense “as far as” (distance limit). رسید is the simple past “he reached/arrived.”
segments.s163-g4: ز گردان = “from the troop.” فرستاده‌ای is the indefinite/participle form meaning “a messenger/an envoy” (here a noun), and برگزید is past “he chose/selected.” The phrase is straightforward: he selected a messenger out of the troop.
segments.s163-g5: ب + دان here appear as a prepositional/purpose phrase (classical orthography) introducing purpose: ‘to/so that (he might)…’. تا explicitly marks purpose here “so that/in order that.” رساند (past of رساندن) means “to convey/deliver (a message).” به شاه آگهی = “to the king the information/news.”
segments.s163-g6: که introduces the content clause “that …” (the thing being reported). گرسیوز آمد = “Garsivaz came/arrived.” Here the following ب + دان (tokenized) function together as an older locative/pointing form meaning roughly “to that/there” (not the modern verb sense). فرهی is an epithet meaning “cultured/distinguished” and modifies the referent (the place or person to which Garsivaz came).
segments.s163-g7: به کشتی = “onto/into the ship.” به یکروز here means “in one day / within a day.” بگذاشت (past) is “he put/placed” and in nautical context means “he launched (the ship) into the water”; آب names the medium ‘water’ that the ship was set upon.
segments.s163-g8: بیامد is past “he came.” سوی بلخ = “toward Balkh.” دل پر شتاب is a common poetic state-of-mind formula: دل “heart” + پر “full (of)” + شتاب “haste/urgency,” i.e. “his heart was full of haste.” Word order places the directional verb before the destination (verb then complement) and finishes with the emotive phrase.
segments.s163-g177: بدانست is a past-tense verb 'he knew/realized' used to introduce what follows. کش here appears as an element tied to the noun کار (an older or poetic form) and links to کار as a modifier; کار دشوار گشت is the core clause 'the work/affair became difficult', with گشت (become) as the copular/phase verb. Word order is subject-less: verbum (بدانست) + clause describing the recognized fact.
segments.s163-g178: This is two short predicative clauses. جهان تیره شد is 'the world grew dark' (جهان subject, تیره predicate adjective, شد 'became'); then بخت او خوار گشت repeats the same pattern with possessive او 'his' and گشت again meaning 'became'. The two verbs (شد / گشت) are simple past/resultative in narrative sequence.
segments.s163-g179: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. برادرش is a noun with the possessive clitic -ش 'his brother'. با خواسته is a prepositional phrase 'with a request/wish' modifying the coming; the preposition با precedes its noun.
segments.s163-g180: This noun phrase describes the company he brought: بسی functions as an adverbial/intensifier ('much/many' in older usage), خوبرویان is the plural 'the fair-faced/beautiful ones', and آراسته is a predicative/adjectival past participle 'adorned' agreeing after the noun. The order is adverb + noun + adjective (typical Persian modifier order).
segments.s163-g181: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the content of the request. زنهار is an interjection/noun 'znehâr' (a warning/plea, literally 'take heed'); خواهد is the present-future stem meaning 'he wants/asks' and governs the following verb; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', and شاه جهان 'the king of the world' is the object/source of the requested action. The construction خواهد + present-form verb expresses desire or asking that something be done by another.
segments.s163-g182: سپارد is the present-form of the verb 'to entrust/hand over' used here in the subordinate clause governed by خواهد ('that he entrust...'). بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him'. تاج و تخت is a standard paired expression 'crown and throne', and مهان is a modifier of تخت (an old/poetic epithet). The sequence shows an object (تاج و تخت مهان) following the verb سپارد.
segments.s163-g183: بسنده کند is a verbal phrase where بسنده (sufficient/content) combines with کند (do/let) to mean 'let him be content' or 'he should be satisfied'. زین جهان literally 'from this world' (i.e. 'with this world') marks the limiting scope, and مرز خویش 'his own boundary' states the limit: object + possessive. The verb phrase is hortative/optative in sense.
segments.s163-g184: بداند is the present/subjunctive form of 'to know' (here used as a predictive/result clause: 'he will know'). همی is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/so', and پایه و ارز خویش is a coordinated noun phrase 'his foundation and worth' (پایه 'basis/standing', ارز 'value/worth') with the possessive implied by خویش. Word order places the verb before the emphasized result phrase.
segments.s163-g113: وگر is a conditional particle ('if' / 'but if') introducing a hypothetical. گویم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of گفتن ('I say' / 'should I say') used here to introduce reported speech. از من گروگان مجوی is an embedded negative imperative/subjunctive clause: از = 'from', من = 'me' (object of از), گروگان = 'a hostage', مجوی = negative present/subjunctive / prohibitve 'do not seek / do not ask'. Word order places the verb گویم before the quoted command clause.
segments.s163-g114: دروغ آیدش is literally 'a lie comes to him' with the verb آید ('comes') plus the enclitic ـش ('to him'). سر به سر here is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'altogether' / 'completely' or 'one thing after another'; it links to the following nouns. گفت و گوی is a paired nominal phrase ('talk and saying' / 'talk and tale'), treating speech as mere words — together the phrase says that everything amounts to talk and false tale.
segments.s163-g115: فرستاد باید places the verb فرستاد ('to send') together with the modal باید ('must' / 'it is necessary'), a word-order that in poetry makes the obligation explicit ('one must send' / 'it must be sent'). بر او indicates the target ('toward / to him') and نوا is a noun meaning 'melody' or 'sound' (here: a musical signal).
segments.s163-g116: اگر starts a conditional 'if'. بی = 'without' and گروگان = 'hostage'; together بی گروگان = 'without a hostage'. ندارد is the present of داشتن ('has not' / 'does not have') used here idiomatically with روا ('permissible / proper') — ندارد روا = 'it is not proper/permissible' (i.e. 'it is not right'). The subject ('for him') is implied from context.
segments.s163-g117: بران سان is a fixed poetic pair meaning 'in that way' / 'thus' (بران = 'thereon/thence', سان = 'like/as'). که introduces the subordinate clause describing what follows. رستم همی نام برد: همی is the classical habitual/progressive marker ('would' / 'kept on'), نام برد is the verbal phrase 'to name / to mention' (نام = 'name', برد here combines to form 'to mention'), so the clause means 'as Rostam kept on naming/mentioning'.
segments.s163-g118: ز = poetic 'of/from' (introducing the group counted). خویشان نزدیک = 'close/near relatives' (نزدیک here means 'near' or 'approximately' depending on construction). صد بر شمرد is an idiom: صد = 'a hundred' (here approximate), برشمرد = 'he enumerated / counted up' (بر + شمرد forming a prefixed verb meaning 'counted up'), so the whole segment says he counted about a hundred near relatives.
segments.s163-g119: بر شاه ایران = 'to the Shah of Iran' (بر used as 'to/toward' in poetic diction). فرستادشان is the past-tense verb 'he sent them' with the object suffix ـشان 'them' attached to فرستاد. The verb therefore directs the previously enumerated relatives toward the king.
segments.s163-g120: بسی here functions adverbially as 'many' / 'much' (intensifier). خلعت = 'robes of honour' (a customary gift); و = 'and'; نیکوی = 'good / fine' (adjective; here qualifying an implied set of gifts); دادشان is 'gave them' (داد + suffix ـشان 'them'). Together: he gave them many robes and good/fine things.
segments.s163-g9: فرستاده آمد is a simple past narrative clause: subject (فرستاده, 'the messenger') + past verb آمد ('came'). به درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the court of the king' (به = to/towards, درگاه = royal court, شاه = king) placed after the verb as the goal/direction.
segments.s163-g10: بگفتند is a reported-speech / impersonal past ('they said' / 'it was said') introducing what follows. گرسیوز آمد به راه is the reported content: a nominal clause meaning 'Garsivaz came on the road' or idiomatically 'Garsivaz was on his way' (به راه = en route).
segments.s163-g11: سیاوش گو is a classical quotative construction: the name + گو ('said' / quotative). پیلتن را is the direct object with the accusative marker را. بخواند is a third-person past narrative form here meaning 'he had (someone) called / he ordered that [Piltan] be called' (the listed sense 'to call/summon').
segments.s163-g12: وزین is here best read as the fused sequence 'و زین' ('and from this'), introducing what follows; داستان is the noun 'story/tale'. چند گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in several ways / in a few manners'. براند is an older third-person past form of راندن ('to drive/lead') used metaphorically: 'he carried/led the tale' → 'he related it / set it forth' in various ways.
segments.s163-g13: چو ... آمد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when', followed by the subject گرسیوز and the past verb آمد ('came'). به درگاه شاه again marks the destination 'to the king's court'.
segments.s163-g14: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said (by the king)'. تا introduces a purposive/result clause here ('so that/in order that'), برگشادند is a past plural form 'they opened' (بر + گشاد + ـند), and راه is the object 'the way/path' — together: 'he ordered that they open the way.'
segments.s163-g15: سیاووش ورا دید is SOV order typical of older Persian: subject (Siyavash) + object pronoun ورا ('him' / 'to him') + past verb دید ('saw'). بر پای خاست is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'stood up / rose to his feet' (بر = on/upon, پای = foot, خاست = rose).
segments.s163-g16: بخندید is past tense 'he laughed' (or smiled). و is the coordinating 'and'. بسیار here is an intensifier 'greatly / much', so بسیار پوزش بخواست functions as 'he asked/pleaded for many apologies' — بخواست is past 'he requested/asked' and پوزش = 'pardon/apology'.
segments.s163-g97: برافگند is a past-tense/perfective verb placed before its subject in inverted poetic word order; it here means 'sent forth' or 'cast off.' گرسیوز is the subject following the verb. اندر زمان is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the time,' giving the temporal frame for the action.
segments.s163-g98: فرستاده‌ای is a noun 'a messenger' (formed from the verb stem) that functions as the object of برافگند; چون is a simile 'like/as'; هژبر دمان is a personal epithet/name (compound) modifying the messenger. The whole phrase describes the kind of messenger sent.
segments.s163-g99: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' with بدو (to him) + گفت (said); word order places the indirect object/adverb first. خیره منه is an admonition: خیره 'astonished/dazed' and منه here functions as a negative imperative 'do not' (an archaic negative), and سر به خواب literally 'head into sleep' = 'fall asleep' (the preposition phrase به خواب expresses motion/state into sleep).
segments.s163-g100: برو is an imperative 'go!'; تازیان is the destination/target (the Arabs) in the plural; نزد + افراسیاب means 'to the presence of / before Afrasiab.' The verb is a simple command directed at the addressee.
segments.s163-g101: بگویش is an imperative 'tell him' (بگو + encliticِ ش for 'to him'); که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that...'; من تیز بشتافتم is the reporting clause with subject من and the past-tense verb بشتافتم 'I hurried,' modified by the adverbial تیز 'swiftly/keenly.'
segments.s163-g102: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/just' or continuous marker) here strengthening the statement; هرچ 'whatever' is a free relative; جستم is past 'I sought/searched' (here sense 'search'); همه یافتم is a compact clause 'I found all' with همه as object 'all/everything' and یافتم 'I found' in past tense.
segments.s163-g103: گروگان is the direct object 'a hostage/pledge'; همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ('now/still/indeed') attached to the main verb; خواهد is third-person present/future 'he wants/is asking for' (poetic tense can imply present habitual or immediate desire); از شهریار marks the source 'from the king.'
segments.s163-g104: چو introduces a conditional 'if'; خواهی here is second-person 'you wish/you want' (present), followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که 'that' and the verb برگردد 'he may return/turn back' (subjunctive/optative sense in a conditional). از کارزار 'from the battle' gives the source/location he would be returning from.
segments.s163-g57: This clause is a jussive/optative expression with the subject omitted: گروگان (object 'a hostage') appears before the verb, then فرستد is a literary 3rd-person form of فرستادن used here as 'let him send' or 'he should send'. به نزدیک ما is a prepositional phrase 'near/to us' (به + نزدیک + personal pronoun).
segments.s163-g58: کند is the 3rd-person jussive/subjunctive of کردن used causatively ('let him make'); روشن is a predicative adjective 'clear/bright' placed after the verb. The noun phrase این رای تاریک ما ('this counsel/plan of ours that is dark/obscure') is the object/compliment of 'make clear'. Word order is verb + predicate-adjective + demonstrative + noun + modifier + possessor.
segments.s163-g59: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'must not / should not'. که introduces the subordinate clause explaining what must not happen. از ما غمی شد is literally 'a grief became from us' — Persian uses شدن + noun to mean 'a sorrow come upon us' ('we be saddened'). ز (poetic form of از) بیم = 'because of/from fear'.
segments.s163-g60: همی is an adverb marking continuous or habitual action ('still/ever'). طبل is the object 'drum' and سازد is the 3rd-person literary form of ساختن used here as 'he makes/plays' (i.e., 'beats'). به زیر گلیم is a locative phrase 'under the kilim' (به + زیر + object). The clause states a continuing action contrasted with the previous admonition.
segments.s163-g61: چو = 'when'. این کرده باشیم is a perfect/subjunctive construction: کرده (past participle) + باشیم (1st-person-plural subjunctive/auxiliary) = 'when we have done this' or 'after we do this.' نزدیک شاه is an adverbial phrase 'near the king' (preposition-less proximity construction common in classical style).
segments.s163-g62: فرستاده here functions as 'a messenger/one sent' (a noun), followed by the modal باید ('must/ought to be'). یکی نیک‌خواه is the appositive/identifier 'a certain well-wisher' or 'someone well-disposed' — i.e. 'a trusted/benevolent envoy.' The overall construction gives a nominal predicate: 'a well-wishing envoy must be sent.'
segments.s163-g63: برد here is a jussive/imperative form meaning 'let him/her/one take/bring' (classical literary usage of بردن). زین سخن = 'of this speech/this matter' (ز + این + سخن) — 'news of this.' نزد او = 'to him / in his presence' (نزد used as a dative/locative). آگهی functions as 'report' or 'information' and can be read as the intended content to bring.
segments.s163-g64: مگر here introduces a purposive/hypothetical sense ('perhaps'/'lest'/'unless' depending on reading) — it signals the hoped-for effect. مغز ('brain/mind') is the object that گرداند (3rd-person verb 'turn, cause to turn' from گرداندن) acts upon. از کین تهی is a prepositional complement 'empty of hatred/vengeance' (تهی as predicate meaning 'devoid of'). The clause thus means 'so that it may turn his mind and make it empty of vengeance.'
segments.s163-g33: Subject phrase: غلامان همه (all the servants) + a following prepositional/strumental phrase introduced by با. Word order is noun + quantifier ('همه') then the instrument/attire marked by با ('with hats and belts'). کمر here means 'girdle/belt' rather than 'waist'.
segments.s163-g34: Continues the description of the servants: پرستنده functions adjectivally/participially (devoted/adoring) and is followed by another with‑phrase with با listing companions/ornaments. طوق زر = 'a collar/torque of gold'. The second با simply coordinates further accompaniments.
segments.s163-g35: پسند آمدش is an impersonal / experiencer construction: 'it pleased him' (پسند آمد + enclitic ش = 'to him'). سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'strongly/very' modifying the following verbal phrase بگشاد روی ('opened his face' = 'broke into a smile'). Word order: verb phrase follows the modifier and the affected noun (روی).
segments.s163-g36: A sequence of actions linked by و. نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction where نگه (gaze/watch) combines with کرد ('did/made') to mean 'he looked'. Then و + بشنید 'and he heard' followed by پیغام اوی where اوی is the possessive ('his message').
segments.s163-g37: Direct‑speech introduction: Tهمتن (proper name/epithet) + بدو ('to him') + گفت ('said'), where گفت introduces the ensuing speech. یک هفته شاد is the temporal phrase ('for one week') plus the predicative adjective شاد ('merry'), forming part of the quoted injunction.
segments.s163-g38: همی باش is an imperative ('be!') with همی as an archaic/intensifying particle ('ever/indeed/may you'), so 'be continually ...'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of time/purpose ('until/so that') and is followed by a subjunctive/optative verb آریم ('we bring' / 'may we bring'). یاد at the end is an admonitory noun 'remember' or 'keep in mind' attached to the whole command.
segments.s163-g39: بدین خواهش is a prepositional phrase 'with/to this request' (بدین = به + دین in older poetic orthography) that introduces the topic. اندیشه باید بسی is a clause with اندیشه ('thought') as subject and باید ('it is necessary / must') as modal; بسی ('much/enough') is the predicative adjective indicating degree: 'this request needs much thought.'
segments.s163-g40: همان نیز ('the same also') links to the previous sentence and prescribes an action: پرسیدن ('to ask' as an infinitive/noun) followed by از هر کسی ('from every person'). Grammatically, this is an instruction connected to the modal necessity expressed earlier: 'also ask the same of everyone.'
segments.s163-g105: فرستاده آمد بدادش پیام is an older/poetic word order: فرستاده (subject) + آمد (past tense 'came') followed by بدادش پیام (= 'gave him a message'). بدادش is a contracted past form where the prefix ب- attaches to داد(ن) 'gave' and ش = 'to him'; here it functions as a past transitive verb phrase 'gave him'.
segments.s163-g106: ز شاه و ز گرسیوز نیک‌نام: repeated poetic preposition ز (= 'from') marks the sources. نیک‌نام is a postpositive adjective ('well‑reputed') that modifies (at least) گرسیوز; the whole phrase = 'from the king and from Garsivaz, the well‑reputed.'
segments.s163-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause uses archaic/poetic V‑initial order: گفت (past 'said') appears before its subject فرستاده; then بشنید شاه places the past verb 'heard' before the subject 'the king'. Overall: 'When the messenger spoke, the king heard.'
segments.s163-g108: فراوان is an adverbial/degree word ('much/repeatedly') modifying بپیچید (past of پیچیدن 'to turn'). The coordinating و links the next clause گم کرد راه, where کرد is the causative/auxiliary 'made/did' and راه is the direct object: literally 'made the way lost' = 'lost (his) way.'
segments.s163-g109: همی is a durative/iterative particle ('kept / continually'), so همی گفت = 'he kept saying'. صد تن ز خویشان من is a partitive/genitive phrase 'a hundred of my kinsmen' with ز marking 'of/among'.
segments.s163-g110: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'thus' or 'in this way'. کم گردد is a stative/future‑oriented form ('become few/diminished') with گردد (subjunctive/occasional present of گشتن/گردیدن). از انجمن = 'from the assembly' (از marks separation/source).
segments.s163-g111: شکست (noun 'defeat') is the subject and اندر آید is the verb phrase 'enters/comes into' with اندر = 'into/within'. بدین بارگاه is a demonstrative phrase 'into this court' (بدین = 'to/into this' in older usage); note the lexical segmentation in the data splits بدین into ب + دین, but here the unit means 'to this'.
segments.s163-g112: نماند is the negative existential 'remains not / there will not remain'. بر من = 'for me' (dative/benefactive sense). کسی نیک‌خواه = 'anyone who is a well‑wisher'; in the negative context کسی functions as 'no one'. The whole clause: 'no well‑wisher will remain for me.'
segments.s163-g129: به نزد سیاوش بیامد is a straightforward prepositional + verb clause: به نزد + NAME = “to the presence of NAME,” followed by the past-tense verb بیامد “came.” The short phrase چو گرد that follows is temporal: چو = “when/as,” and گرد here is poetic/archaic and functions adverbially (from گردیدن / گرد) indicating approach/arrival or simply linking to the following clause (“when/once [he]…”). In other words, the first part states the motion (he came to Siyavash) and چو گرد introduces the time circumstance for the next clause.
segments.s163-g130: شنیده سخنها همه یاد کرد is a past-tense result clause: شنیده is a perfect/passive participle from شنیدن used here as a temporal/causal participle (“having heard” / “when he heard”), followed by the object سخنها “the words/speeches” (plural marked by -ها), the adverbial همی/همه “all/entirely,” and the main past verb کرد (here ‘he did/remembered’ with یاد as its object: یاد کردن = “to remember”). Word order is participle/nominal object → adverb → compound verb (یاد کرد).
segments.s163-g131: بدو گفت is an inversion common in Persian poetry: بدو = “to him” (prepositional pronominal clitic + preposition fused) + گفت (past of گفتن) = “he said to him.” چون کارها گشت راست is a temporal/clausal subordinate: چون = “when/as,” کارها = “the matters/affairs,” گشت = past of گشتن “became/turned,” and راست = “right/settled.” So the clause reads “when the affairs became/right/settled.” Note گفت is the main reporting verb and what follows is reported content.
segments.s163-g132: چو گرسیوز ار بازگردد رواست is the content of what was said and combines two conditional particles: چو = “when/if/as,” followed by the name گرسیوز (Garsivaz). ار = an archaic/poetic conditional particle meaning “if/supposing” (similar to modern اگر). بازگردد is subjunctive/present imperfective form meaning “he returns” (here used under the conditional), and the predicate رواست = روا + ست = “is fitting/allowed.” The whole clause: “if/when Garsivaz should return, it will be fitting (allowed).”
segments.s163-g133: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning “he ordered/commanded/said (to do so).” تا introduces a subordinate clause of result/purpose here = “that/so that,” followed by خلعت “the robe of honour” as direct object and آراستند = past plural “they adorned/prepared” (آراستن used transitively here). The clause therefore means “he commanded that they prepare the robe (be adorned).”
segments.s163-g134: سلیح و کلاه و کمر خواستند is a simple nominal list (three items joined by و) followed by the plural past verb خواستند “they requested/wanted/asked for.” Word order is typical Persian: list of items (object) + verb. کمر here means “girdle/belt” (a war-belt), and سلیح = “arms/weapon(s).”
segments.s163-g135: یکی اسپ تازی به زرین ستام is a compact noun phrase listing a gift: یکی = “one” (a single item), اسپ تازی = “Arabian steed” (اسپ = horse, تازی here denotes Arabian/Arabian-horse), then به + زرین + ستام indicates accompaniment/ornament: به زرین = “with golden-…” and ستام is a poetic noun describing the trappings/ornaments (here functioning as the thing that is golden). The construction is noun + modifier sequence typical of epic listing.
segments.s163-g136: یکی تیغ هندی به زرین نیام follows the same pattern: یکی = “one,” تیغ هندی = “an Indian sword” (تیغ = sword, هندی = Indian/or of Indian manufacture/style), and به زرین نیام = “in/with a golden scabbard” (نیام = scabbard). The preposition به marks the accompaniment/setting (the scabbard that is golden), producing “an Indian sword in a golden scabbard.”
segments.s163-g41: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. بشنید is a past 3rd‑person singular verb 'he heard' that anchors the clause in the past. گرسیوز is the proper name (the subject) and پیش + بین form the compound پیش‌بین 'seer' (an appositive/title placed after the name), so the whole phrase reads 'when Garsivaz, the seer, heard'.
segments.s163-g42: زمین را is the direct object marked by را ('the ground/the earth'); ببوسید is past 3rd‑singular 'he kissed'. و simply coordinates the next verb. کرد is the light verb 'did/made' used with the noun آفرین to mean 'uttered/praised', so کرد آفرین = 'he uttered “Afarin”/he praised.'
segments.s163-g43: یکی before خانه functions as an indefinite/partitive 'a certain/one (house)'. او را marks the house as possessed/definite ('his house' as the object). بیاراستند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they adorned/they dressed (it)', so the clause means 'they adorned one of his houses.'
segments.s163-g44: به + noun here indicates the material/covering ('with' or 'in'): به دیبا = 'with brocade'. و is the conjunction 'and'. خوالیگران is a rare/obscure noun (left untranslated in the glossary) naming another material or ornament. خواستند is past 3rd‑pl 'they wished/desired' and here signals their intention in furnishing the house (they provided/desired it so).
segments.s163-g45: نشستند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb 'they sat' (here 'they took their seats/they sat [down]'). بیدار is an adjective 'awake' qualifying their state. هر دو = 'both (of them)' and به هم = 'together/with one another', so the phrase reads 'both sat awake together.'
segments.s163-g46: سگالش is a rare/uncertain noun (here functioning as the object) and گرفتند is past 3rd‑pl 'they took' or 'they seized' — together meaning 'they took/held (that) سگالش' (i.e., they held a discussion/counsel). بر introduces the domain 'about/concerning' and بیش و کم literally 'more and less' = 'great and small matters' (idiomatically: pros/cons or important and trivial points).
segments.s163-g47: ازان کار 'from that affair' gives the cause; شد is the past copula/auxiliary 'became' and links to the predicate that follows. پیلتن is the proper name (the patient/subject of the resulting state) and بدگمان is the predicate adjective 'suspicious/distrustful' — together: 'Pīltan became suspicious because of that affair.'
segments.s163-g48: کزان is a fused prepositional phrase 'from which/from that' and گونه here means 'manner/way' (i.e., 'in that manner' or 'because of that course of events'). گرسیوز is the name and آمد is the past 'came'; دمان is a place‑word 'hem/skirt/outskirts' indicating where he arrived. Grammatically this reads 'and in that way Garsivaz came to the outskirts.'
segments.s163-g121: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause (so that/that) giving the content of the order. کوس با کره‌نای lists the instruments (کوس = drum, با = 'with') that will be used in the subordinate clause; the verb that completes the clause follows in the next segment.
segments.s163-g122: زدند is third‑person plural past 'they struck/sounded' (here used for sounding instruments) and is the main verb of the تا‑clause. و links a second coordinated action. فروهشت functions as a verbal/adjectival past participial action 'spread out' or 'set out (curtains)' and پرده‌سرای is its object/affected noun 'pavilion/curtain‑hall' (noun+noun phrase).
segments.s163-g123: به is the preposition 'to/toward' introducing a list of places. The following words are a simple coordination of place names (خارا, سغد, سمرقند, چاچ); word order is a standard enumerative sequence joined by و ('and'). Note these are proper‑name items in a prepositional phrase.
segments.s163-g124: This continues the place/list phrase. سپیجاب is another place/name (here treated as a listed locality). و links to آن کشور 'that country' (آن as a demonstrative 'that/the'), and then تخت عاج is a noun‑noun combination 'throne (تخت) of ivory (عاج)'—تخت is the head noun and عاج specifies its material.
segments.s163-g125: تهی کرد is a compound meaning 'made empty' or 'emptied' (تهی + کرد, with کرد as light verb 'to make/do'). و links to the next clause. شد with با سپه سوی گنگ here means 'he departed/started' or 'he went' — شد serving idiomatically for movement, followed by the prepositional phrase با سپه ('with the army') and سوی گنگ ('toward Ganga' — a destination).
segments.s163-g126: نجست is a past verb with the negative prefix ن‑, here meaning 'he did not seek' (i.e., he made no attempt to find an excuse). The nouns بهانه, فریب, درنگ are coordinated objects ('an excuse, deceit, or delay') of that negative verb; و simply joins the nouns.
segments.s163-g127: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از followed by رفتنش ('his leaving') expresses 'concerning/of his departure' (the genitive/oblique use of از). رستم is the subject who becomes آگاه ('aware'); شد is the past auxiliary 'became' (i.e., 'Rostam became aware'). Note the possessive enclitic ‑ش on رفتن = 'his going'.
segments.s163-g128: روانش 'his soul/mind' is the subject (روان + possessive ‑ش). ز (poetic variant of از) means 'from/because of' and links to اندیشه 'thought'. کوتاه شد literally 'became short' — an idiomatic expression meaning his mind/heart was relieved or curtailed of care; شد is the past 'became'.
segments.s163-g81: This is a conditional protasis introduced by اگر (if). The phrase زیر نوش اندرون is a compact, slightly inverted description: زیر (beneath/in) + نوش (the drink) + اندرون (inside) — together meaning “in/inside the drink.” The clause ends with زهر نیست (there is no poison), where نیست is the negative copula placed after the predicate, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s163-g82: دلت را is the object phrase: دل (heart) + -ت (your) with را marking it as definite/emphatic object. ز رنج و زیان is a prepositional phrase 'from/by suffering and loss.' بهر نیست is a predicative idiom meaning 'there is no cause/need' — literally 'for (بهر) there is not,' so the whole clause says your heart has no reason for pain or loss.
segments.s163-g83: چو (when/if) introduces a temporal/conditional clause: پیمان همی کرد is a habitual/punctual verbal phrase ('made/does make a pact') with همی marking the epic habitual/present force. خواهی درست addresses the addressee: خواهی (you wish/will) here has an exhortative/optative sense ('be willing' / 'intend') and درست modifies it as 'rightly' or 'uprightly.' The clause is somewhat elliptical and sets up the reason clause introduced by که.
segments.s163-g84: که introduces the following explanatory/result clause. آزار و کینه are the objects 'harm and hatred.' نخواهیم جست is a negative verbal phrase 'we will not seek' (نخواهیم as the negated auxiliary/volitive, جست = 'seek' here). Note the object(s) precede the verb, as typical in Persian.
segments.s163-g85: ز گردان 'from among the troops/regiments' uses ز (from) with گردان (troop). The relative clause که رستم بداند همی follows: که (that/who) introduces the relative, رستم is the subject, بداند is 'knows' (third-person), and همی again is the habitual/epic marker indicating 'knows (well/constantly).'
segments.s163-g86: کجا (where) begins a subordinate interrogative/relative notion: نامشان 'their names' (نام + شان), بر تو is the dative 'to you' (بر = toward/to), and خواند همی is the verb phrase 'he calls/reads (out) (habitually)' with خواند as third-person and همی marking continuous/habitual action. The clause thus asks or states where he will call/recite their names to you.
segments.s163-g87: بر من فرستی is an imperative/subjunctive request 'send to me' with بر + dative object (من). فرستی is second-person singular. به رسم نوا is an adverbial phrase 'in the custom/manner (رسم) of melody (نوا)' — literally 'in the customary melody' (رسم here = manner/style).
segments.s163-g88: که باشد به گفتار تو بر گوا is a purpose/result clause: که = 'so that/in order that,' باشد is subjunctive/optative 'may be' (let it be), به گفتار تو = 'to/for your speech/words,' and بر گوا = 'as a witness' (گوا = witness; بر marks the role/relationship). The whole clause: 'so that it may be a witness to your words.'
segments.s163-g153: ببرم is a 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form from بردن (here 'take/leave, carry off') meaning 'I will take/leave'. زمین‌گر is a fused poetic form where زمین ('earth, land') combines with the conditional particle -گر ('if'); read together as 'if (it is) the earth' or more idiomatically 'if (you) order me to go to the earth / leave the land'. تو فرمان دهی is a subordinate conditional clause: تو (you) + فرمان (command) + دهی (present/subjunctive 'you give/issue'), literally 'if you give the command'.
segments.s163-g154: ز رفتن نبینم همی جز بهی is a single clause giving the result/assessment: ز (poetic 'from') + رفتن (verbal noun/infinitive 'going, departure') marks 'on/at leaving'; نبینم is negative present/subjunctive 'I shall not see' (i.e. 'I shall see nothing'); همی is an archaic/poetic particle adding emphasis or continuous aspect; جز بهی literally 'except good' = 'nothing but good'. The whole means 'on leaving I shall see nothing except good.'
segments.s163-g155: سیاوش (proper name) is the subject. ز گفتار او is a prepositional/causal phrase: ز (poetic 'from/because of') + گفتار (speech, utterance) + او (his): 'from his speech'. شاد شد is past 'became happy' (شاد = happy, شد = became/auxiliary), so 'Siavash became glad at his words.'
segments.s163-g156: حدیث فرستادگان is a noun phrase 'the report/tale of the envoys' (حدیث = tale/report, فرستادگان = envoys). باد شد literally 'became wind' — a poetic metaphor meaning the news 'went forth like the wind' or 'spread rapidly'. شد is the simple past/copula 'became'.
segments.s163-g157: سپهدار بنشست is past tense 'the commander sat down' (سپهدار = commander, بنشست = sat down). و رستم به هم links Rostam to that action: و (and) + رستم (Rostam) + به هم (together/with him) — here به هم means 'together/with one another' (به is the prepositional marker and هم gives the sense 'together').
segments.s163-g158: سخن راند is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'they spoke' or 'uttered words' (سخن = speech; راند here functions as a verbalizing/past action 'uttered/spoke', though its literal senses are 'drove/expelled' — poetic usage = 'set words forth'). هرگونه از بیش و کم is a modifier: هرگونه 'of every kind' (here 'in every way/kind'), از (of/about/concerning) + بیش و کم ('more and less' = 'greater and lesser matters' i.e. all matters). Together: 'they talked about every sort of thing, large and small.'
segments.s163-g159: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/ordered' (classical courtly form). تا here introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that/that' (i.e. 'he ordered that...'). رفت پیشش دبیر literally 'the scribe went before him' (رفت = went; پیشش = before him / in his presence; دبیر = the scribe/secretary) and in subordinate construction means 'that the scribe should go to him.'
segments.s163-g160: نوشتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to write' or 'writing'. یکی نامه‌ای is 'a certain/a single letter' (یکی = a certain/one, نامه‌ای = a letter — the -ای ending is partitive/indefinite). بر حریر is a prepositional phrase 'on silk' (بر = on/upon, حریر = silk). Grammatically this completes the purpose: 'to write a letter on silk.'
segments.s163-g273: سیاوش is the proper name (here vocative: the poet addresses Siyâvash). چو is the conditional/temporal conjunction 'when'. پیروز is a predicate adjective 'victorious'. بودی is the past tense 2nd-person form 'you were' (classical/epic usage addressing the hero). بجنگ is a fused prepositional phrase (ب + جنگ) meaning 'to/into battle' (infinitival/directional). Together the clause reads 'Siyâvash, when you were victorious in battle...'.
segments.s163-g274: برفتی is the past 2nd-person verb 'you went/you departed' (continuing the address to Siyâvash). بسان is a simile marker 'like/as'. دلاور is an epithet 'valiant, brave' modifying the following noun. پلنگ is the simile's noun 'leopard' (a figurative epithet). The phrase means 'you went like a valiant leopard.'
segments.s163-g275: چه is the interrogative 'what'. جستی is the past 2nd-person 'you sought' (here meaning 'did you seek'). جز 'except' introduces the exception/list. از is the preposition 'from/of' used with the list that follows. تخت و تاج و نگین are the coordinated nouns 'throne and crown and signet' — items being named as what was sought.
segments.s163-g276: تن آسانی is a compound of تن 'body' and آسانی 'ease' = 'bodily ease' or 'personal comfort'. و connects this phrase to the next. گنج ایران‌زمین is a genitival phrase 'treasure of the land of Iran' (گنج modified by the place-name ایران‌زمین). These continue the list of things the speaker says were being sought.
segments.s163-g277: همه یافتی is a terse clause 'you obtained/got everything' (همه 'all' + یافتی past 2nd-person 'you obtained'). جنگ is the noun 'war/battle' (here the domain in which he gained things). خیره modifies جنگ as 'dazzling/astonishing' (literally 'staring/astonished' used adjectivally). مجوی is an admonitory negative imperative 'do not seek'. The clause strings an assertion ('you gained all') with a prohibition about further seeking of showy battles.
segments.s163-g278: دل روشنت = 'your bright/clear heart' (دل 'heart' + روشنت = روشن + enclitic -ت 'your'). به here means 'into/toward' (directional). آب تیره 'dark water' (آب 'water' + تیره 'dark/ominous'). مشوی is a negative imperative-form (classical) meaning roughly 'do not become/go' or 'do not let it be so' — here idiomatically 'do not let [your heart] be turned/sullied.' The whole clause warns against sullying the hero's bright heart with dark waters (figuratively: despair, shame or corruption).
segments.s163-g279: گر is the conditional 'if'. افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrâsiâb' (subject of the conditional). این سخنها 'these words' (این 'these' + سخنها plural 'words/speeches'). که is the relative/conjunction 'that/which' introducing a clause modifying سخنها. گفت is the past verb 'he said/said' (here 'which he said'); the provided sense-list gives 'conversation' rather than 'said', so the exact verbal sense is the past tense 'he said' (sense not present in the supplied list).
segments.s163-g280: به before a verbal noun here marks the manner/means: 'by/through' (بِه + شکستن = 'by breaking'). پیمان 'covenant/treaty' is the object of شکستن (شکستن the infinitive/masdar 'breaking' or 'to break'). بخواهد is the present/future/subjunctive 3rd-person 'he wants/would wish'. نهفت is an adjectival/predicative participle 'hidden/concealed' used as the complement of بخواهد ('he would wish [it] concealed'). Together: 'if Afrâsiab would wish to have those words concealed by breaking the treaty'.
segments.s163-g209: وزان is a literary particle meaning 'thereupon' or 'from that (cause)'. روی here refers to 'countenance / appearance' (literary use), so وزان روی together sets up a consequential 'thereupon, with that appearance'. چون is the comparative 'like / as', introducing the following simile; رستم شیرمرد is the name plus epithet ('Rostam, the brave'), the epithet following the proper name as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s163-g210: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. بر with motion here means 'to / before' (motion toward the king). شاه ایران is the object ('the King of Iran'). The final چو گرد is a simile ('like a dust-cloud'); چو 'like' precedes گرد 'dust' (poetic image), so the simile is postposed to describe the manner of his coming.
segments.s163-g211: به پیش اندر آمد is a compact motion phrase: به پیش 'forward / into the presence' + اندر آمد 'came in'. بکش کرده دست is a participial construction: the verbal root بکش 'draw/pull' combined with کرده (perfect participle) modifies the subject, meaning 'having drawn (his) hand' or 'with his hand drawn'. The order places the participial phrase after the motion clause.
segments.s163-g212: برآمده is a past participle 'having risen / having appeared' placed before سپهبد (the commander) as a modifier. ز جای نشست is a simple past clause 'rose from (his) place and sat' where ز جای 'from his place' gives the point of motion and نشست is the past tense 'sat' (or 'took a seat').
segments.s163-g213: بپرسید is the past tense 'he asked' (classical form). و is the coordinating 'and'. بگرفتش is formed from the prefix/preterite stem ب- + گرفت 'seized' with the enclitic object -ش 'him', so 'he seized/took him' (i.e. led him aside). اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'to the side / aside', showing the direction of the action.
segments.s163-g214: ز here functions as a preposition 'about / concerning' (poetic ז/ز). فرزند is 'son/child' (the topic asked about). و 'and' links the next phrase; از گردش روزگار is another prepositional object 'about the turning/rotation of time' i.e. 'about the vicissitudes of fate' where گردش روزگار is a genitive compound 'turns of time/fate'.
segments.s163-g215: This is a series of prepositional topics introduced by ز/از 'about' (poetic order). ز گردان 'about the troops', و از رزم 'and about battle', و کار سپاه 'and the affairs/deeds of the army' — کار here means 'affairs' or 'matters', and سپاه 'army' is in apposition, so the whole lists the subjects of inquiry.
segments.s163-g216: وزان 'and from that' links back to the foregoing topics. تا here introduces the extent/point of questioning ('as to / namely'), followed by چرا 'why'. بازگشت is used as the past verbal sense 'he returned / he turned back' and او is the subject pronoun 'he'; ز راه 'from the road' gives the origin/route. The clause asks why he had turned back from the road.
segments.s163-g137: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." The clause follows classical Persian order with the verb دید (saw) at the end: "When Garsivaz saw that robe of the king." آن (that) modifies خلعت (robe), and شاه (king) is a genitival modifier of خلعت.
segments.s163-g138: تو گفتی is subject + past verb ("you said"); the remainder is a rhetorical question introduced by مگر ("could it be that/perhaps/isn't it that"). بر زمین ماه دید places the preposition بر before its noun phrase ("on/to the ground the moon") with the verb دید at the end; the implied sense is "(did he) see the moon on the ground?" — i.e. "Could the moon have been on the ground?"
segments.s163-g139: بشد is a past/result or narrative verb meaning roughly "it happened/he became" (here introducing the following action); it governs the following manner phrase. با زبانی پر از آفرین is a prepositional phrase of manner: با + زبانی ("with a tongue/with words") where پر از constructs "full of" and آفرین means "praise/acclamation." Together: "he (did so) with words/tone full of praise."
segments.s163-g140: تو گفتی again is "you said." مگر introduces a rhetorical possibility as in the previous line. بر نوردد زمین has the preposition بر followed by the verb نوردد and its object زمین; نوردد here means "to shine upon/illuminate" (third-person form), so the clause reads roughly "(you) said, 'Might it not shine upon the earth?'"
segments.s163-g141: نشست is the past tense verb "sat" (third person). The classical poetic sequence از بر تخت عاج functions as a locative: از بر + [تخت عاج] — here meaning "upon/on the ivory throne." تخت (throne) is modified by عاج (ivory).
segments.s163-g142: بیاویخته is an adjectival/participial form meaning "loose-hanging/dishevelled." It heads the phrase بیاویخته بر سر عاج تاج, where بر سر (on the head of) locates تاج (crown), modified by عاج (ivory). The whole phrase: "an ivory crown hanging loosely on his head."
segments.s163-g143: همی is a classical continuous/iterative particle (narrative marker) often glossed "kept on/was"; رای زد is an idiom meaning "to take counsel/offer an opinion" (رای = counsel/plan, زد is the light verb forming the idiom). با یکی چرب‌گوی is a prepositional phrase "with a certain smooth‑tongued (flatterer)" where یکی = "a certain/one" and چرب‌گوی = "smooth-tongued, flattering."
segments.s163-g144: کسی کاو is an archaic relative construction: کسی = "someone" and کاو functions as a relative "who/that." سخن را دهد رنگ و بوی is a verb phrase where دهد (gives) takes سخن را (the speech) as its object and رنگ و بوی ("color and scent") as the things given — idiomatically "one who makes the speech vivid/ornamental."
segments.s163-g313: This clause places temporal adverbial words first: هم اندر زمان ("at the same time/then") + object طوس را (Tūs with the definite object marker را) before the verb خواند. شاه appears after the verb as the subject; خواند here is past tense meaning "summoned/called." The word order (object before verb and postposed subject) is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s163-g314: بفرمود is an honorific/past-form verb meaning "he commanded/ordered." The following infinitive/masdar construction لشکر کشیدن (literally "to draw/move the army") expresses the action ordered, and به راه means "onto the road/toward the way." Together they form the second half of the sentence: he ordered the army to proceed on the road.
segments.s163-g315: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." بیرون شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning "came out/stepped out" (شد here functioning as the past verb), and از پیش کاووس literally means "from before Kavus," i.e. "from the presence of Kavus." The final طوس is the subject of the clause (Tūs).
segments.s163-g316: بفرمود again is the past/honorific verb "he ordered/commanded." تا here introduces the content clause (equivalent to English "that"/"so that") and governs a subjunctive/optative sequence to follow; the list لشکر و بوق و کوس names the things he ordered (the army, the trumpets, and the war-drum).
segments.s163-g317: بسازند and کنند are plural subjunctive/prescriptive forms (here dependent on the preceding تا): they express what was ordered — "that they make/prepare" and "that they arrange." آرایش ره means "the array/arrangement of the march/way" (آرایش = arrangement/array, ره = poetic/older form of راه meaning "way/march"). The two verbs form a coordinated instruction.
segments.s163-g318: وزان (from و + زان) means "by that/from that" and links the result clause: رزمگه راه کوته کنند is a result expressed with کوته کردن ("to make short/shorten") applied to راه رزمگه "the road/path to the battlefield." کنند is again the subjunctive/plural form matching the ordered action — "that they shorten the way to that battlefield."
segments.s163-g265: بمانید is the 2nd-person plural imperative of ماندن (“stay/remain”). تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning “until.” او بیاید uses the subjunctive بیاید after تا to mean “he comes” (i.e. “until he comes”); the pronoun او precedes the verb as usual. به جنگ is a prepositional phrase “into/to the battle” that specifies the destination of his coming.
segments.s163-g266: که here introduces an explanatory clause (“for/that”). او خود places emphasis on the subject (literally “he himself”). شتاب آورد literally ‘bring haste’ (شتاب = haste, آورد = bring) and functions idiomatically as a verbal phrase meaning “will hasten” or “make haste.” بی‌درنگ is an adverb “immediately/without delay,” modifying شتاب آورد and placed after it for emphasis.
segments.s163-g267: ببودیم is an archaic past 1st‑person‑plural form meaning “we were.” یک چند is an adverbial phrase meaning “for a while / somewhat.” در جنگ is the locative prepositional phrase “in battle,” and سست is the predicate adjective “weak,” so the clause reads “we were for a time weak in battle.”
segments.s163-g268: در آشتی is “in (the) reconciliation/truce.” او is the subject “he,” and گشاد is a predicate adjective meaning “loose / slack / lax.” از نخست is a temporal phrase “from the first / from the outset,” modifying the clause to indicate that the laxness existed from the beginning.
segments.s163-g269: کسی is the indefinite “someone.” کاشتی is an older past form of کاشتن; here it functions impersonally or narratively (“one planted / there was planting”). جوید is a present/epic form “seeks” or “would seek.” سور و بزم are two nouns (“feast and banquet / revelry”) joined by و and are the objects/associations of seeking.
segments.s163-g270: نه negates the following predicate. نیکو بود is the copular phrase “was good” (بود = was). پیش رفتن is the infinitive/masdar “to go forward / to advance,” and برزم is a poetic term indicating involvement with the fight/struggle (i.e. ‘into battle’); overall the clause says “it was not good to go forward into battle.”
segments.s163-g271: و دیگر opens an additional point (“and another / moreover”). که is the subordinating conjunction “that.” پیمان شکستن is a nominalized verbal phrase “the breaking of the covenant” (شکستن = to break used as a noun), and ز شاه uses poetic ز to mark the agent/source (“by the king”), so the phrase means “the king's breaking of the pact.”
segments.s163-g272: نباشد is the negative copular/subjunctive form meaning “is not / should not be” in this evaluative formula. پسندیده is a participle/adjective “pleasing / acceptable,” and نیک‌خواه (“well-wisher” or benevolent one) names the person to whom the action is displeasing; together the clause means “is not pleasing to a well-wisher.”
segments.s163-g241: شما‌گر here functions as an archaic/poetic conditional marker equivalent to 'if you' (literally 'you‑if'); خرد را is the direct object (خرد 'wisdom' with the object marker را after it). بسیچید is a past/preterite or perfective verb 'muster/gather' (classical Persian verb form) whose object is خرد را; کار at the end names the affair/task — the word order is typical Persian: (conditional) + object + verb + summary noun.
segments.s163-g242: نه introduces a negation/contrast ('no' / 'not so'). من is the first‑person subject 'I'. سیرم is a stative predicate 'I am sated/tired' (present perfective stative: 'I am weary'). از + noun marks source/causation ('of/from war'); the repeated از ... و از ... enumerates two related causes: جنگ and کارزار (both mean war/battle).
segments.s163-g243: به نزد + name is the classical locative/benefactive 'to the presence of / to (Siavash)'. فرستم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive form meaning 'I send / I will send / I shall dispatch' (classical narrative can use it for an immediate future). کنون 'now' is a sentence adverb modifying the action.
segments.s163-g244: یکی مرد is 'one man / a certain man' (one + noun). پردانش is an adjective after the noun meaning 'learned, well‑informed' (classical Persian permits the adjective to follow the noun). و links a second quality; پرفسون is an archaic or rare epithet (here used as a second descriptor — likely 'pious/devout' or another quality), so the phrase means 'a certain learned and p‑r‑f‑s‑o‑n man.'
segments.s163-g245: بفرمایمش is a 1st‑person verb with a suffixed object pronoun ـمش ('him') and here means 'I will tell/order him' (classical polite/imperative construction). ک is a clipped form of که 'that' introducing the commanded content. آتشی (here written together with the k) + کن is an imperative 'make/produce a fire' (کن 'do/make' imperative); بلند is an adjective 'high/tall' describing the fire. Word order: verb of saying + که + object clause (imperative) + adjective after noun.
segments.s163-g246: ببند is an imperative 'bind/close/fasten'. گران پای is a compound adjective 'heavy‑footed' (گران 'heavy/weighty' modifying پای 'foot' to describe gait or burden), followed by the noun ترکان 'the Turks' (the patient of the verb). The final ببند repeats the imperative for emphasis — a common poetic device. The overall order is imperative + descriptive phrase + object + reiterated imperative.
segments.s163-g247: برآتش بنه is an imperative phrase 'put on the fire' (برآتش = 'on/onto the fire', بنه = 'put/place' in an imperative sense here), followed by خواسته هرچ هست 'whatever is desired/they desire' (خواسته as a noun/participle 'that which is wanted', هرچ 'whatever', هست 'is'). The clause thus orders to place on the fire whatever is wished; the sequence (prepositional phrase + verb + relative phrase) is characteristic of classical diction.
