segments.s163-g248: نگر is an imperative 'look! / see!'. تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that / to see whether'. نیازی in context functions as a noun phrase 'a need' or the verb 'there is need' (classical texts often use such forms elliptically). به یک چیز 'for/of any thing' specifies the object of need. دست here appears in an idiomatic sense related to 'come into hand / be needed' — the whole clause means 'see whether there is any need for anything.'
segments.s163-g145: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase fronted for emphasis ('from the host'). همی + جست is an emphatic/progressive verbal complex common in epic Persian: همی gives immediacy or emphasis and جست is the past/simple verb 'leapt/sprung'. گردی سوار is a nominal phrase 'a mounted/wandering rider' (گردی modifies سوار), placed after the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s163-g146: که introduces a relative clause ('who/which'). با او is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with him'. بسازد is the present/subjunctive-form of ساخت used here in the sense 'makes/causes/engages', followed by دم شهریار, a genitival phrase 'the king's breath' (دم = a moment/attention). The clause therefore describes what that rider can do to the king.
segments.s163-g147: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus (he) said'. با او marks the indirect object 'to him'. گو is an imperative ('say'/'tell') directed to the addressee, followed by the name پیلتن as its object: 'tell Piltan'. Word order places the imperative before the person to be told, a poetic but normal construction.
segments.s163-g148: کزین در is a locative phrase 'from this gate/door' (کزین = 'from this', در = 'gate/door'). که introduces a subordinate clause explaining what happens at that gate: یارد (here 'helper/assistant') is the agent, گشادن (infinitive) 'to open/reveal' is the verb, and سخن 'speech/conversation' is its object — 'from which the helper will open/reveal the speech'.
segments.s163-g149: همانست is a copular nominal clause 'is the same'. کاووس is the subject. کز پیش بود is a relative/temporal phrase 'as he was before' (کز = 'that/from which', پیش بود = 'was before'), so the whole segment states that Kavus remains unchanged from his former state.
segments.s163-g150: ز تندی 'by/through haste' is the topical prepositional phrase. نکاهد is a negative-present/old-form verb meaning 'does not lessen' (here negated), نخواهد is the future/modal auxiliary 'will not', and فزود 'increase' is the lexical verb — together conveying that haste will neither diminish nor increase him (i.e. it will not change him).
segments.s163-g151: مگر here has the limiting sense 'unless'. من شوم is subjunctive/optative 'that I may become/come' and with نزد شاه جهان ('in the presence of the king of the world' — شاه جهان is a compound title) it expresses the speaker's action 'unless I appear before the king'. The clause sets a condition for the following instruction.
segments.s163-g152: کنم آشکارا is 'I will make (it) clear/openly' (کنم = 'I do/make', آشکارا = 'openly'). برو is an imperative 'go', and بر نهان 'toward/into hiddenness' (بر = 'toward', نهان = 'hidden') is the direction — so the speaker orders the addressee to go and stay concealed unless the speaker himself appears before the king and makes things public.
segments.s163-g193: 'وزان' is an archaic/poetic compound meaning 'from that' or 'on that account' (causal link to the previous line). 'روی' here is not a literal 'face' but part of the idiom (hence glossed as a locution); 'گرسیوز' is a proper name; 'نیک‌خواه' is an epithet 'well-wisher' describing Garsivaz. The whole segment is the introductory noun phrase giving who comes and why.
segments.s163-g194: 'بیامد' is simple past 3rd-person 'came'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: the verb appears before the agent/phrase that follows. 'بر' is a preposition here indicating motion toward or incidence upon (i.e., 'to/against the king'), 'شاه توران' is 'the king of Turan', and 'سپاه' is 'army' — the phrase says an army came to/against the king.
segments.s163-g195: 'همه' = 'the whole/entire' modifies 'داستان' (story/tale), and 'سیاوش' identifies whose story it is. 'بگفت' is an older past-tense form meaning 'he told/related' (object phrase preceding verb is normal in Persian poetic order).
segments.s163-g196: The particle 'که' introduces the content clause ('that ...'). 'او را' places 'him' in the accusative/emphatic position (here standing for Siavash or the praised man). 'ز شاهان' is a poetic way to mean 'of/among the kings' (ز = 'from/among'). 'کسی نیست جفت' is a negative existential: 'there is no one (کسی نیست) a match/pair (جفت)'; syntactically 'جفت' (match/pair) functions as the predicate. Overall: 'that there is no one among the kings who is his equal.'
segments.s163-g197: 'ز' is the preposition 'from' indicating source or reason. 'خوبی دیدار' = 'the goodness/beauty of his appearance/meeting' (in context 'appearance' or 'the way he looks'), coordinated with 'کردار او' = 'his deeds/conduct'. The possessive 'او' (he) attaches to the second noun but is understood to apply to the first as well; items are listed as sources of praise.
segments.s163-g198: Another stacked 'ز' phrase listing inner qualities: 'هوش' (intellect), 'دل' (heart/mind), 'شرم' (here 'modesty' rather than raw 'shame'), and 'گفتار' (speech). The repeated conjunctions 'و' simply link the qualities; the final 'او' again refers back to the same person.
segments.s163-g199: A string of adjectival epithets: 'دلیر' = 'brave', 'سخن‌گوی' here 'eloquent' (not just 'speaker'), 'گرد' in this poetic list conveys movement/roving ('going about') rather than the literal 'dust', and 'سوار' = 'horseman' or 'mounted warrior'. The conjunctions join the attributes in series.
segments.s163-g200: 'تو گویی' is an idiomatic impersonal phrase meaning 'one would say' or 'it seems as if' (here introducing a figurative statement). 'خرد دارد' = 'he has wisdom' (دارد = present-tense 'has'). 'اندر کنار' = 'at/in his side' i.e. 'beside him' — together: 'you would say wisdom stands at his side' (i.e. he is wise).
segments.s163-g257: بیاید is the 3rd-person present-subjunctive/optative of آمدن used here as a wish/jussive ('may he come'). بجنگ is the subjunctive/infinitival form of جنگیدن functioning as the verbal complement ('to fight'). The object pronoun تو follows the verb phrase and Afrasiab is the patient/name; word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb first, then infinitive phrase and object).
segments.s163-g258: چو ... گردد is a conditional/time clause: چو = 'when/if' and گردد (from گشتن/شدن) is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive meaning 'becomes' or 'happens' ('when he comes/it happens'). برو here is an imperative/jussive-like or poetic intransitive use meaning 'become/go into' a state; it heads a series of predicative adjectives (ناخوش، آرام، خواب) that describe the resulting condition. The adjectives are listed after the verb in a compressed, poetic stretch.
segments.s163-g259: تهمتن is the speaker's name/epithet (vocative/subject). بدو is the indirect-object clitic phrase meaning 'to him' (به او). گفت here is the past verb 'said' (positioned before the vocative phrase in the line's word order). کای شهریار is a vocative: کای = 'O' and شهریار = 'king'.
segments.s163-g260: دلت is دل + enclitic ت meaning 'your heart'; را is the definite/object marker attaching after the accusative phrase. بدین is the fused form of به + این ('to/at this'), introducing the phrase کار ('matter/affair'). غمگین مدار is an archaic/poetic negative imperative: مدار (here) = 'do not make' (مگذار/مکن), so 'do not make (your heart) sad.' The object (دلت را) precedes the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s163-g261: سخن is the direct object 'a word'/'words' placed before the imperative بشنو ('listen'/'hear') for emphasis. از من marks source ('from me'). تو‌ای شه is a vocative phrase 'O king' (تو‌ای is an emphatic archaic 'you, O ...', شه = 'king'). نخست ('first') is an adverb modifying the imperative phrase.
segments.s163-g262: پس آنگه is a redundant pair of temporal markers ('then, afterwards') common in poetic diction. جهان is the subject 'the world'. زیر فرمان is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'under (the) command', and تست is the archaic/copular form meaning 'is yours' (originally توست). Thus the predicate says 'the world is under your command.'
segments.s163-g263: تو گفتی که = 'you said that' (تو = subject; گفتی = 2nd-person past 'you said'; که is the complementizer 'that'). بر جنگ افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase with بر + جنگ meaning 'in/against (in the) war with Afrasiab' — بر indicates opposition/target in this martial context.
segments.s163-g264: Here مران is a vocative/proper name ('Maran') addressed by command. تیز لشکر is a noun phrase where تیز (adjective 'swift') modifies لشکر ('army/host'). بران is an imperative (archaic/poetic) meaning 'drive/send' (imperative of بردن or similar); روی آب = 'onto the water' (روی = 'on/upon', آب = 'water') marks the direction/terrain for the action.
segments.s163-g249: پس is a connective 'then/therefore' at the clause start. The phrase follows the usual Persian SOV/order for an imperative: object + را (آن بستگان را) is marked as definite/accusative and فرست is an imperative verb 'send' (classical short form of بفرست). بر + من here functions as a directional prepositional phrase 'toward/to me'.
segments.s163-g250: که introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause ('so that/that'); من is the subject 'I'. بخواهم is the present/subjunctive/intentional form meaning 'I will demand / I shall have', here marking the speaker's intention. ز + تن‌شان ('from their bodies') uses poetic ز = 'from' and شان is the third-person possessive clitic on تن; گسست is used as 'separation/severing' (a noun/nominal verbal sense) so the whole phrase means 'that I may have their heads separated from their bodies.'
segments.s163-g251: تو با لشکر خویش is a prepositional/possessive phrase 'you with your army'; خویش is formed from خوی + possessive ش and here functions as 'your/one's own' by context. سر پر ز جنگ is a nominal phrase where سر 'head' is modified by پر ز جنگ 'full of war/battle' (پر + ز 'full of' + noun), a poetic adjectival construction placed after the noun.
segments.s163-g252: برو is the imperative 'go'. تا introduces the following phrase as a purpose/result/time marker ('so that / until'), here giving direction/goal. به درگاه او is the prepositional phrase 'to his court/presence' (درگاه = royal court/presence) and بی‌درنگ is an adverb meaning 'immediately/without delay' placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s163-g253: همه (everyone) is the addressed plural; دست بگشای is an imperative phrase 'open/extend your hand(s)' where دست is literal 'hand' and بگشای is the imperative of گشایش/opening. تا یکسره introduces the result/purpose 'so that / altogether / completely' with یکسره functioning as an adverb 'completely' or 'all at once.'
segments.s163-g254: چو here means 'when' (a temporal conjunction). گرگ اندر آید is a simple clause 'a wolf comes in' (اندر = in/into, آید = comes). به پیش بره is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of the lamb' (به پیش = toward/in the presence of; پیش here 'before') and functions as the destination/target of the wolf; together the clause implies the wolf will fall upon the lamb.
segments.s163-g255: چو at the start is conditional 'if'. تو is the explicit second-person subject. سازگیری is a verbal-noun meaning 'arrangement/organization' or 'arming/preparation'; بد modifies it as 'badly' or 'ill-made'. آموختن here is used as a verbal noun ('learning'/'being taught') and together the phrase has the sense 'if you make/allow bad arrangements or training' (i.e., 'if you prepare poorly/teach badly').
segments.s163-g256: سپاهت is سپاه + enclitic ت 'your army'; کند is the present/simple verb 'does/makes' (classical usage can indicate future/result). غارت و سوختن are verbal-noun objects 'plundering and burning', so the clause means 'your army will engage in plunder and burning.'
segments.s163-g281: هم از جنگ جستن نگشتیم سیر = literal word order is emphatic/poetic: هم (also/even) + از جنگ جستن (from seeking war) + نگشتیم سیر (we did not become sated). نگشتیم is past/perfective with negation meaning 'we have not become' (from گَشتن 'become'), and سیر is a predicative noun 'sated'. The clause therefore means 'we too have not become satiated in seeking war.'
segments.s163-g282: بجایست شمشیر و چنگال شیر = بجایست is an impersonal/adjectival expression meaning 'it is fitting' or 'appropriate'; the following noun phrase 'شمشیر و چنگال شیر' ('sword and the lion's claws') is the thing judged fitting. The order places the predicate before the concrete instruments for poetic effect.
segments.s163-g283: ز فرزند پیمان شکستن مخواه = 'ز' (poetic 'with/regarding') introduces the object 'فرزند' (son/child) and the verbal-noun phrase 'پیمان شکستن' (breaking the covenant). 'مخواه' is a negative imperative 'do not desire/ask for.' Together: 'Do not desire to break the covenant with the son.'
segments.s163-g284: مکن آنچ نه اندر خورد با کلاه = 'مکن' is the simple negative imperative 'do not (do)'. 'آنچ' = 'that which/what', and the phrase 'نه اندر خورد' means 'does not fit/suit' (اندر = in/with; خورد here functions idiomatically as 'befit/enter' in older usage). 'با کلاه' ('with the hat/crown/helmet') specifies the standard of fitness: 'do not do anything that would not befit the crown.'
segments.s163-g285: نهانی چرا گفت باید سخن = This combines two closely linked ideas: 'نهانی چرا گفت' is an interrogative 'Why was it said secretly?' (نهانی = secretly; چرا = why; گفت = said/uttered), and 'باید سخن' uses باید 'must/necessary' with سخن 'word/speech' to assert that the matter must be spoken plainly. Word order is poetic and compressed.
segments.s163-g286: سیاوش ز پیمان نگردد ز بن = 'سیاوش' is the subject. 'ز پیمان' = 'from the covenant' (i.e. with regard to the treaty) and 'نگردد' (negated subjunctive/imperative sense of گردیدن 'turn/change') means 'will not turn away / will not change.' 'ز بن' = 'from the root/foundation' (emphatic). The clause says 'Siyâvash will not abandon the covenant at its root.'
segments.s163-g287: وزین کار کاندیشه کردست شاه = 'وزین' (weighty/grave) modifies 'کار' (affair/matter). 'کاندیشه کردست' is a compound: کـ- (prefix forming a deverbal noun) + اندیشه 'thought' + کردست (archaic perfect 'has done/has taken'), so 'has given thought to.' The subject 'شاه' (the king) appears at the end, a normal poetic order: 'The king has pondered this weighty matter.'
segments.s163-g288: بر آشوبد این نامور پیشگاه = 'بر' (upon/at) + آشوبد (past/resultant of آشوبیدن 'become disturbed/agitates') describes an affective reaction; the following noun phrase 'این نامور پیشگاه' ('this renowned presence/court') is what is described as agitated. Poetic inversion places the verb before its subject: 'At this, the famed court/presence was thrown into turmoil.'
segments.s163-g17: ببوسید is a past tense verb (he kissed) placed before the subject (poetic word-order: V + subject). گرسیوز is the subject. از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar / at a distance' modifying the action. خاک is the direct object 'dust/earth' (i.e. kissing the ground).
segments.s163-g18: This is two coordinated nominal phrases describing appearance and feeling. رخش (here with the enclitic -ش = 'his') means 'his countenance/face'; پر ز + noun is a fixed poetic pattern meaning 'full of (something)' (literally 'full from ...'). Thus رخش پر ز شرم = 'his face full of shame.' و links the two halves; دلش (heart + possessive) پر ز باک = 'his heart full of fear.'
segments.s163-g19: سیاووش is the subject. بنشاندش is a past-tense transitive verb 'he seated him' where the final -ش is the object pronoun 'him' attached to the verb. زیر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'under/beneath the throne' giving the place of seating.
segments.s163-g20: This clause continues the action: از + noun + بپرسید = 'asked about ...'. Here از marks the topic 'concerning Afrasiab' (asked questions about Afrasiab). بپرسید is past tense 'he asked' and سخت is an adverb/adj modifying how the asking was done — 'harshly/firmly'. (Poetic compression leaves the agent understood from previous clause.)
segments.s163-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بنشست is past 'sat down.' In poetic word order the verb can precede or surround the subject; here گرسیوز is the person who sat. از گاه نو means 'at/from the new time' (i.e. 'at the new hour' or 'on the new occasion'), setting the temporal frame for what follows.
segments.s163-g22: بدید is past 'he saw.' آن is the demonstrative 'that.' The phrase سر و افسر (here written together as آن سر وافسر) means 'that head and commander / that chief and officer' — a compound title; شاه نو 'the new king' is in genitive relation: 'that chief of the new king.' Note: in the supplied tokenization 'وافسر' appears joined and the lemma list gives different senses, but in context it should be read as و افسر = 'and afsar/commander.'
segments.s163-g23: به رستم is the indirect object 'to Rostam' (the person spoken to). چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'); in Persian poetry the speaker's name often comes after the verb, so کافراسیاب at the end is the subject 'Afrasiab' who is speaking.
segments.s163-g24: چو is the temporal 'when.' از تو خبر یافت is a compact causative/past construction meaning 'he learned/heard news of you' (از = 'concerning/of', تو = 'you', خبر = 'news', یافت = past of 'to find/obtain' = 'learned/got'). اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'in haste' — together: 'When he heard of you, he hurried.'
segments.s163-g217: نخست is an adverb meaning 'first/initially'. از سیاوش is a prepositional phrase where از means 'about/concerning' (classical usage). زبان برگشاد is a poetic collocation: زبان 'speech/utterance' plus برگشاد, the past of برگشودن 'to open', together meaning 'began to speak' (the verb برگشاد follows the noun in this inverted, literary order).
segments.s163-g218: ستودش is the past verb 'praised' with the enclitic -ش 'him' attached (verb+enclitic object). فراوان here functions adverbially 'greatly/abundantly' modifying ستودش. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter' and بداد is an archaic past form meaning 'gave' (نامه بداد = 'gave a letter'); grammatically this is the object (نامه) followed by the past verb (بداد) in the expected SOV order of classical Persian.
segments.s163-g219: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is the object 'the letter'. برو خواند is a split/archaic verbal sequence: the prefix بـ/بر combined with خواند (past of خواندن) — together meaning 'read it' or 'read aloud'; the prefixed element appears separated in the verse. فرخ دبیر is the subject (name + title) placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s163-g220: رخ شهریار جهان is a genitive chain 'the face of the king of the world' (رخ = face). شد is the past of شدن 'became'. چو is the simile marker 'like/as', and قیر is the noun 'tar/pitch' — so رخ ... شد چو قیر = 'his face became like tar'. Word order places the predicate (شد) before the simile phrase, standard in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s163-g221: به رستم چنین گفت = 'he said thus to Rostam' (به marks the indirect object). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so', and گفت is the past verb 'said' (here followed by the reported clause). گیرم is a common classical idiom meaning 'let us suppose' / 'I grant' (a subjunctive/hypothetical use), followed by که introducing the subordinate clause. اوی is an archaic/poetic third-person pronoun 'he'.
segments.s163-g222: جوانست is the copular past/present expression 'is young' (جوان + است contracted). و is 'and'. بد نارسیده is a compound adjectival phrase: بد here intensifies/qualifies (classically 'ill-/not-') and نارسیده means 'not ripe/not yet arrived' = 'not yet mature'. بروی is a verbal form from رفتن ('to go/arrive') used here in the sense 'has arrived/come' in negative sense with نارسیده; together the phrase means 'not yet come/mature'. Grammatically this is a predicate clause describing 'he'.
segments.s163-g223: چو here functions as a conditional 'if'. تو نیست is the negative copula 'you are not' (classical present used for counterfactual). اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = in/within). سر به سر is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'throughout' / 'at all' (emphatic: 'anywhere/at all in the world'). Word order places the conditional clause first, as typical.
segments.s163-g224: به جنگ is a locative/directional phrase 'to/into battle'. از تو is the object phrase indicating who is sought ('you' — از marks the person sought/concerned). جویند is the present/plural verb 'they seek/search for' (agreeing with شیران). شیران هنر is the subject at the end: شیران 'lions' figuratively 'brave men/warriors', هنر 'skill/virtue' specifying their quality ('heroes of prowess'). This SOV/Verb-Subject ordering is poetic inversion.
segments.s163-g297: تو (you) is the addressee (poetic 'thou'); ایدر is an older/dialectal form meaning 'here'; بمان is the 2nd-person singular imperative/present stem of ماندن 'remain/stay'. تا introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'until ...'. سپهدار طوس is a title + name (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry: 'the commander Tus').
segments.s163-g298: ببندد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of بستن used in narrative sense 'sets in motion/arranges' (classical usage with ب-). برین is orthographic fusion of بر + این 'on/for this' so برین کار = 'this affair/enterprise' (کار is the direct object). بر پیل کوس is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'by/with the elephant's drum' or 'the elephant-drums being beaten' (بر = on/upon; پیل = elephant; کوس = war-drum).
segments.s163-g299: من is the 1st-person pronoun 'I'; اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; هیونی (an unusual/archaic noun) here denotes a fierce/savage person or creature sent as agent/object; فرستم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/present-stem of فرستادن 'I send/I will send'; به بلخ is the prepositional phrase 'to Balkh'. Word order is Subject — temporal adverb — object — verb — destination.
segments.s163-g300: یکی functions as an indefinite determiner 'a certain' (introducing the letter as one item); نامه is 'letter'; با is the comitative preposition 'with'; سخنهای is the plural/genitive-like form of سخن 'words/speech' (here 'words'); تلخ is literal 'bitter' used figuratively 'harsh, bitter' describing the contents.
segments.s163-g301: سیاوش is the proper name 'Siyâvash'; اگر introduces the conditional 'if'; سر here is literal 'head' used idiomatically in the construction 'سر ... ز بپیچد' = 'turn one's head away from' i.e. 'break away from'; ز is the archaic/pre-classical form of از 'from'; پیمان من = 'my covenant/pledge' (poetic omission of ezāfe linking).
segments.s163-g302: بپیچد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of پیچیدن 'to turn' and here completes the idiom 'سر ... ز بپیچد' = 'turn away/break faith'; نیاید is negative present/subjunctive/optative 'will not come' (classical narrative meaning 'shall not'); به فرمان من is a prepositional phrase 'at/by my command' meaning 'obey me'. The two verbs mark condition (if ... then) and result.
segments.s163-g303: بطوس in this poetic spelling represents ب + طوس 'to Tus' (the preposition fused with the name) or simply the name in the role of indirect object; سپهبد is the military title 'spahbed/commander'; سپارد is 3rd-person singular present of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'; سپاه is the direct object 'army'. The clause literally: 'He entrusts the army to Spahbed Tus.'
segments.s163-g304: خود here is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'himself' (the implied subject); و = 'and'; ویژگان is the plural 'the distinguished/selected (men)' (i.e. his chosen followers); باز is the adverb 'again/back'; گردد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن/گشتن 'turn/go/return'; به راه 'to the road/way' indicates direction. Together: 'he himself and his chosen men return to the road.'
segments.s163-g233: شما را: the object (you) is marked by the definite-object marker را and appears before the predicate, as is common in Persian. بدان (here written as ب + دان): an archaic/poetic prepositional/ demonstrative element meaning 'for/unto/with that' (literally 'to that'), linking what follows to a particular quality. مردری: an abstract noun 'manliness/valor' describing the quality for which 'you' are desired. خواسته: a past-participial/predicative form 'desired/wanted' that functions here as the main predication ('you have been desired'); the implied agent is omitted (typical of Persian passive or verbal adjective uses).
segments.s163-g234: بدان گونه (ب + دان + گونه): a fixed adverbial phrase 'in that way/manner' (بدان = 'in/with that' + گونه = 'manner'). بر شد: a classical compound where بر (an upward/intensive element) + شد ('became/rose') yields 'rose up'/'was exalted' or 'became so'. دل آراسته: the noun phrase 'the adorned/attired heart' is the grammatical subject (دل) modified by the participle آراسته; the clause says that this heart 'rose' or 'was uplifted' in that manner.
segments.s163-g235: کجا بستد: a rhetorical question 'where did (he) cease/refrain?' (کجا = 'where'; بستد is an older/poetic perfective/past form meaning 'ceased/refrained/shut off'). از هر کسی: 'from every/any person' where از marks separation or source and هر کسی means 'everyone/anyone'. بی‌گناه modifies کسی and means 'innocent' — together 'from any innocent person'.
segments.s163-g236: بدان تا: 'with that, so that' — دان (here part of بدان) + تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'). بپیچیدتان: verb بپیچید (ب- prefix + پیچید 'turn') with enclitic تان 'you (pl.)' = 'that (it) turn you'; the b- prefix marks the verb form common in Persian. دل: the subject of the subordinate clause 'the heart'. ز راه: 'from the path' (ز = poetical form of از). Together the clause means 'so that the heart would turn you away from the road/path' (i.e., mislead or divert you).
segments.s163-g237: به صد ترک: the preposition به plus a numeral phrase 'a hundred Turks' — here به gives the direction/target notion often translated 'at' or 'against' or simply 'to' (classical Persian allows this flexibility). بیچاره و بدنژاد: two adjectives following ترک; بیچاره 'unfortunate/poor wretch' and بدنژاد 'low-born/ignoble' describe those Turks.
segments.s163-g238: که: a relative/relative-pronoun-like particle 'whose/that' introducing the relative clause. نام پدرشان: a genitive construction 'the name of their father(s)' (شان is the possessive clitic 'their'). ندارید یاد: literally 'you do not have (ندارید) remembrance (یاد)' — an idiomatic way of saying 'you do not recall/remember.' The verb is present (ندارید) negated, so 'whose fathers' names you do not remember.'
segments.s163-g239: کنون: adverb 'now' or 'at present'. از گروگان: 'concerning/of the hostage' (از marks the topic/source). کی اندیشد او: a rhetorical clause 'who would think?' or 'who thinks?' — کی here is interrogative/emphatic 'who' (or 'when' in other contexts), اندیشد is the classical/poetic form of 'think', and او ('he') is the emphatic pronoun placed after the verb.
segments.s163-g240: همان پیش چشمش: 'that very (thing) before his eyes' — همان 'that/the same', پیش 'before/in front of', چشمش 'his eye/his sight' (possessive clitic). همان خاک کو: 'that same dust/earth — where is (it)?' کو is the classical interrogative 'where is?' used here to ask rhetorically about the whereabouts of that dust which was once under his eyes (i.e., what has become of it).
segments.s163-g225: ندیدی is a past negative 2nd‑person (rhetorical) verb 'did you not see' with the subject omitted. بدیهای افراسیاب is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the evils/wicked deeds of Afrasiab' (بدیها plural with the name following), placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s163-g226: که introduces a relative clause ('which'). گم شد is a passive/Resultative 'became lost' ('was lost' / 'was taken away'), with ز ما marking 'from us' (i.e. lost to/from us). خورد is here literal/metaphorical 'ate' (devoured), taking آرام و خواب ('peace and sleep') as its objects; the simple past verbs are arranged compactly in poetic style.
segments.s163-g227: مرا (object/experiencer) precedes the verb in a marked/poetic order. رفت here expresses 'set out / was going', while بایست کردم is a light‑verb construction 'I made to stand' → 'I stopped' or 'I halted' and درنگ is the direct object 'a pause / a moment's delay'. Overall: 'I was going, I stopped and paused.'
segments.s163-g228: مرا بود is an existential/possessive pattern meaning 'there was to me' → 'I had'. با او indicates 'with him / toward him'. سری پر ز جنگ is a noun phrase 'a head (mind) full of war' (سری = head/mind, پر ز = full of, ز جنگ = of war), i.e. 'my mind was full of fighting.'
segments.s163-g229: نرفتم is 1st‑person past negative 'I did not go / I did not leave'. که introduces the reported content/reason ('because' / 'that'). گفتند is the 3rd‑person plural 'they said' (reporting speech). ز ایدر مرو = 'do not go from here' (ز = from, ایدر = here, مرو = negative imperative 'do not go' in classical usage).
segments.s163-g230: بمان is a jussive/imperative 'remain / stay'. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until'. بسیچد is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'musters / assembles (troops)' (third person), and جهاندار نو = 'the new world‑ruler / sovereign'. Together: 'Stay until the new sovereign musters (his forces).'
segments.s163-g231: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بادافره is a compound adjective 'wind‑borne' or 'borne by the wind' modifying ایزدی خواست ('divine will/desire'). خواست بود here is a poetic past construction 'there was a will / a desire (arose)'.
segments.s163-g232: مکافات = 'retribution/punishment', followed by بدها ('the bad/wicked' in genitive), then بدی ('evil' or 'ill') and خواست بود = 'was desired / was willed' — an impersonal/past construction meaning that punishment of the wicked was willed or decreed. The noun sequence is compact and typical of epic/Persian poetic diction.
segments.s163-g305: ببیند is the classical present/subjunctive form with the prefix ب- (here an optative/imperative sense: 'let him see' / 'may he see'). ز من = 'from me' (poetic preposition ز). هرچ اندر خورست is a relative phrase: هرچ (هر چه) 'whatever', اندر 'inside/in', and خورست (adjectival/predicative) = 'is eaten / edible' (so the whole phrase means 'whatever is inside and edible'). The clause as a whole means 'let him see/learn/take from me whatever inside is edible.'
segments.s163-g306: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. او را uses را in a dative/topic-like sense ('for him' / 'as regards him'); چنین 'such/this sort of'; داوری 'judgment, opinion'; در سرست is effectively درِ سر است → 'is in (his) head' (سرست is a contracted/poetic sequence meaning 'is in the head'). The clause therefore reads: 'if such a judgment is in his head.'
segments.s163-g307: غمی گشت is an intransitive copular/verb phrase: غمی (nominal 'grief') + گشت (past of گشتن 'became') → 'became sorrowful'. رستم is the subject. به آواز گفت attaches as a sentence-final phrase meaning 'he said aloud' (به + noun indicating manner 'in voice' + گفت 'said').
segments.s163-g308: که introduces reported speech ('that'). گردون here is the impersonal agent 'the sky / fate / heavens'. سر من = 'my head' (possessive structure), and بیارد is a subjunctive/potential form (بـ prefix + یارد ~ بیاورد sense 'may bring' / 'may bring about'). نهفت is a passive/adjectival root meaning 'hidden, concealed' (poetic/archaic); together the clause expresses a wish/complaint: 'that the heavens may bring (it) and hide my head' — idiomatically 'that heaven may hide/cover my head' (i.e. a piteous wish for concealment/death).
segments.s163-g309: اگر introduces a hypothetical 'if'. طوس is the proper name (here the hero Tus). جنگی‌تر is the comparative form of جنگی 'warlike' (the -تر suffix makes 'more warlike'). از ... رستم is the comparative construction 'more ... than Rostam'. است is the copula 'is'.
segments.s163-g310: چنان دان is an imperative or hortative 'consider it so' (دان here = 'consider / reckon'). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. رستم ز گیتی کم است literally 'Rostam from the world is کم' — idiomatically 'Rostam is scarce/rare in the world.' Here کم has the sense 'scarcely / rare', so the clause means 'think of it like this: Rostam is rare in the world.'
segments.s163-g311: بگفت این = 'he said this' (بگفت past narrative 'he said' + این 'this'). و = 'and'. بیرون شد = 'became outside' → 'went out/stepped out' (شد as auxiliary/verb of motion). از پیش اوی = 'from before him / out of his presence' (اوی an archaic/clitic form referring to 'him'). Altogether: 'He said this and went out from his presence.'
segments.s163-g312: پر از ... = 'full of ...' (پر 'full' + از marking the content). خشم چشم = 'eyes full of anger' (خشم 'anger', چشم 'eye(s)'). و = 'and'. پر آژنگ روی = 'and his face full of آژنگ' — آژنگ is a rare/poetic word describing a hostile or dark colouring/mood of the face; روی 'face'. The whole phrase describes his appearance: eyes filled with wrath and a face showing a dark/hostile expression.
segments.s163-g289: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. کاووس is the subject; بشنید is the simple past 'heard'; شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became' marking change of state; پر ز خشم is a predicative phrase 'full of anger' built from پر + ز + noun (preposition z + خشم). Word order is typical Persian: temporal conj. + subject + verb + result phrase.
segments.s163-g290: برآشفت is a past tense verb meaning 'he became agitated/was enraged' (prefixed بر- intensifies the verb). زان کار = 'because of/from that affair' (زان = از آن). و simply links the next clause, and بگشاد is a past action 'opened' (here used as verb) with چشم as its object. The clauses are coordinated: first clause gives emotional reaction, second clause a physical reaction.
segments.s163-g291: به marks the indirect object 'to Rostam' (dative). چنین is a sentence adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past 3rd-person verb 'said' and شاه جهان ('king of the world') is the speaker; Persian allows the indirect object and reported manner to precede the subject, giving the poetic order 'to Rostam thus said the king of the world.'
segments.s163-g292: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. ایدون here functions adverbially ('henceforth/anymore' in context) combined with نماند 'does not remain' to mean 'will not remain'; سخن is the subject 'speech/word' and در نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret'. Together: 'that henceforth no word will remain hidden.'
segments.s163-g293: که again introduces reported content. این در سر او = 'this in his head/mind' (در سر as 'in the mind'); تو is the topicalized subject 'you', and افگنده‌ای is perfect/past 2nd‑person 'you have put/placed'. The order emphasizes the thing put into his mind before stating the agent.
segments.s163-g294: چنین here links to the previous clause ('thus/so'); بیخ کین is a compound object 'the root of hatred' (بیخ = root, کین = hatred/grudge). از دلش 'out of his heart' (ش = his). کنده‌ای is 2nd‑person perfect 'you have torn out/uprooted'. The clause asserts the consequence of what the addressee has done.
segments.s163-g295: تن آسانی خویش is a nominal phrase 'bodily ease/comfort of yourself' (تن = body; آسانی = ease; خویش = 'one's own', here split orthographically into خوی + ش). جستی is past 2nd‑person 'you sought' (sense 'to seek'); برین = بر این 'for this/in this' (بر + این). The verb jستی governs both the direct object phrase and the following negated contrast.
segments.s163-g296: نه is the negation contrasting what was sought; it negates افروزش here understood as 'the shining/brightness/glory' (abstract noun). تاج و تخت و نگین are a triad of regalia 'crown, throne, and gem/signet' in apposition to افروزش. The negation belongs to the earlier verb: 'you sought your ease, not (the) glory of crown, throne, and gem.'
